Overton's frame and physical profile is NFL-ready and applicable to hybrid schemes through alignment versatility, but the next step in his development will be to develop his rush arsenal and produce more at an independent level. Regardless, Overton's raw talent makes him an immediate rotational boon, and impact starter potential is present down the line.

name: Kevin Coleman Jr.
blurb: Kevin Coleman Jr. is one of the hidden gems of the 2026 NFL Draft. Evaluators will be curious about his background, as he played at four different schools in four collegiate seasons. The reason for his final transfer in 2025 was clear, however: He wanted to return home to St. Louis and play for his hometown team, the Missouri Tigers. Though the Tigers struggled at times in 2025, partly due to subpar QB play, Coleman thrived as the team's most efficient weapon. He caught 66 passes for 732 yards and a score, and the advanced numbers give him an even greater endorsement. Coleman's two-year PFSN WR Impact score of 81.7 is stellar, and per TruMedia in 2025, he logged 2.55 yards per route run, a 13.11% catch rate over expectation, and an exceedingly low 2.5% drop rate. Coleman is undersized, but competes at the catch point with resounding consistency and a verifiable clutch gene, and his separation profile exhilarates on film. He's one of the twitchiest, most fluid movers in the entire class, whose keen understanding of spatial manipulation and tempo enables him to win versus press, decouple easily on double-move routes, and splice his way into open zones. Right away, Coleman is a plus rotational WR with return ability, and he has starting potential as a movement-Z.

name: Landon Robinson
blurb: Landon Robinson was one of the best defenders in his conference across his entire three-year starting career. After riding the bench in 2022, Robinson emerged with four sacks and 4.5 tackles for loss in 2023, and then built on his success in 2024 and 2025. Across that two-year stretch, Robinson was a two-time first-team All-AAC competitor. In 2025, after accruing 6.5 sacks and 8.5 TFLs, he earned first-team All-American honors and recognition as the American Conference Defensive Player of the Year, and was one of the top standouts at the East-West Shrine Bowl. At 5'11" and 291 pounds, Robinson is undersized, but his size affords him elite natural leverage and pad level maintenance, and he has defiant play strength in spite of his lacking measureables. He has the leveraging, lower-body strength, and line-resetting power to hold strong in run defense against solo blocks, and he flashes the ability to split double-teams and combos with knee drops and torque. Meanwhile, as a pass-rusher, he's extremely explosive and agile, and while he can improve his counter consistency, his explosive power element, baseline pass-rush nuance, and red-hot motor solidify his disruptive floor. Equipped to play in both even and odd-front schemes, Robinson can be a high-quality rotational presence right away, with quality starter potential.

name: Ephesians Prysock
blurb: Ephesians Prysock is a resident size-speed freak in the 2026 NFL Draft CB class. Originally a four-star recruit, Prysock began his career at Arizona, and broke out as a starter in his sophomore season with one interception and seven pass breakups. In 2024, he transferred to Washington, following head coach Jedd Fisch, and reprised his role as a starter. He carried that mantle through 2025, totaling 13 pass deflections and an INT in two seasons with the Huskies. Prysock played opposite another size-speed specimen in teammate Tacario Davis, and is the less refined of the two, but his upside is tantalizing, nonetheless. At 6'3", 196 pounds, Prysock has over 33" arms and a wingspan over 80", and he also boasts 4.45 speed and elite explosiveness when trending vertical or clamping down overtop routes. Additionally, he has spindly quickness for his size, and flashes surprising fluidity for his height. There are still times where Prysock drifts too tall in off-man and zone coverage, inducing delays in reduction and plant-and-drive efficiency. In a similar lens, Prysock's technique and targeted physicality remains a sore area in press-man and at stems, and he's not as consistent as desired at tracking and impacting the ball with his length. All this considered, Prysock has a create-a-player mix of speed, quickness, fluidity, and length. He's flashed the ability to play inside and out, he processes well in space, and he can attack blocks with his length. Prysock's floor as a starter is lower than average, but his scheme-versatile ceiling is compelling if he can tighten down his technique in contact and catch-point situations.

name: JC Davis
blurb: JC Davis played defensive tackle in high school, but switched to offensive tackle and immediately showed promise with the Contra Costa Comets at the JUCO level. In 2021, as a freshman at Contra Costa, Davis earned first-team All-CCCAA honors at OT, and leveraged that quick progression into an opportunity at New Mexico. He  served as the Lobos' full-time starting left tackle in 2022 and 2023, then reprised that same role at Illinois in 2024 and 2025. Three times, Davis earned first-team all-concerence honors, at three different levels, and he looks built to contribute on offensive line in the pros. At 6'4" and a playing weight around 330 pounds, with over 34" arms, Davis is a bear in close quarters, with heavy hands, a strong anchor, good natural leverage and knee bend, and a wide frame that helps him control positioning. He's relatively slow-footed and lacks high-end flexibility, and could end up seeing out a transition to guard as a result. He also experiences lapses in upper-lower synergy with premature extensions at times. Still, Davis' extensive collegiate experience has yielded him a strong operational floor, as well as great two-phase awareness. Immediately, he projects as a strong depth piece in gap and inside zone schemes, and he has a degree of starting upside at both tackle and guard, as a relatively immovable presence with line-resetting hand power and physicality.

name: Beau Stephens
blurb: Beau Stephens was a central part of the Iowa Hawkeyes' Joe Moore Award-winning offensive line, and also boasts PFSN's highest individual OL Impact score of the 2025 season, with a grade of 94.1. At around 6'5", 315 pounds, Stephens has above-average size and mass, and is a solid athlete for his size, with very good burst off the line and even better range in space when he's able to open up his strides. Stephens is ultra-physical and tenacious in the run game, and technically sound in pass protection, with very good footwork efficiency, synergetic feel, and awareness. Stephens' sub-32" arms can be a limitation at times; he's powerful in spite of that measurement, but he can be baited into over-reaching and lurching, and his latches aren't as strong against longer opponents. Additionally, in pass protection, Stephens still has room to improve his play strength and anchor footwork, and his balance at engagement is inconsistent. Nevertheless, Stephens passes the desired athletic and intangible thresholds, he's a gnarly competitor who relishes contact, and his flexibility is a surprising trump card that enables him to acquire leverage and engage his base consistently. He projects as an instant depth piece and future starter.

name: Aiden Fisher
blurb: Aiden Fisher was a third-team All-Sun Belt performer as a true sophomore at James Madison in 2023, and was a first-team All-American in both of his two seasons with the Indiana Hoosiers. In 2025, Fisher accumulated 97 tackles, 10.5 tackles for loss, 4.5 sacks, and two interceptions, while earning a solid 78.7 PFSN LB Impact grade and serving as the field general for a Championship-level defense. Fisher won't tip the scales at 6'1", 233 pounds, with middling arm length, nor is he an elite functional athlete. Nevertheless, Fisher is extremely intelligent and tenacious, and those two superlative qualities should help him hold down a starting job in the NFL. Fisher has good baseline burst, quickness, and fluidity, to be sure, but what makes him different is his instant reactive quickness, pre-snap and post-snap processing, gap aggression and take-on physicality, well-versed coverage ability, and blitzing versatility. Fisher's football IQ pits him against the smartest defenders in the class, and he attacks both phases with a technically-sound voracity that underscores his projection. Capable of playing a even-front MIKE or odd-front ILB role, Fisher's ceiling is slightly capped by non-elite tools, but he still has the makings of a quality starter.

name: Anthony Lucas
blurb: Anthony Lucas brings a perplexing evaluation to the fold in the 2026 NFL Draft cycle. A true senior and a former five-star recruit, Lucas passes the eye test with his long 6'5", 272-pound frame and energized athleticism. However, the production and consistency haven't quite come along for him, even in his fourth season. The high-end flashes on tape are extremely enticing. At his size, Lucas has actionable power, stacking strength in the run game, and has shown he can win with inside counters using his lateral agility and heavy hands. Much of his evaluation is ultimately a projection, but he has even and odd-front versatility as a stand-up or hand-in-the-dirt rusher.

name: Cole Payton
blurb: Cole Payton redshirted his first year at NDSU, and from 2022 through 2024, he was the backup to Cam Miller. He was utilized most often as a designed runner, but finally got his shot as a full-time starter in 2025. In his last season of eligibility, Payton passed for 2,719 yards, 16 touchdowns, and four interceptions on over 70% completion, while also running for 717 yards and 13 additional TDs. Additionally, he ended the year with the second-highest PFSN QB Impact score in the FCS (89.7). The crux of Payton's appeal as a prospect is his physical talent. At 6'3", 233 pounds, he's a well-built prototype with elite explosive athleticism, bristling long speed, and a rocket arm that can generate high-end velocity from multiple launch points and angles. But as a one-year starter, he's sorely lacking in-game reps, and his processing runs on the slow side as a result. Payton shouldn't be thrown into the fire right away as a field general, and is a developmental QB first, who could be utilized in certain Taysom Hill-esque packages early on. If he enters the right situation and has time to acclimate before getting needed reps, starting upside is present.

name: Deion Burks
blurb: Deion Burks first emerged as a third-year player at Purdue in 2023, accumulating 47 catches for 629 yards and seven scores. He'd ride that momentum to a transfer opportunity with Oklahoma in the SEC, but injuries relegated him to just five games of action in 2024. In 2025, Burks returned with a vengeance, quickly establishing himself as the top target for QB and fellow draft hopeful John Mateer. Burks racked up 225 yards and two scores in the first three games, and eclipsed the 100-yard mark in a standout performance against the vaunted Michigan defense. At 5'10", 182 pounds, Burks fits the mold of a dual-sided separation and RAC threat. He excels on schemed touches with his explosiveness, speed, and twitch, but he's also a savvy zone navigator who can splice into Cover 2 holes with his range, and make smooth body-control catches.

name: Joe Royer
blurb: Joe Royer is an intriguing mid-round TE prospect with tantalizing receiving upside at the NFL level. Having accrued 938 yards and seven touchdowns over his past two seasons, Royer distinguished himself as a consistent pass-game threat and a primary target for Brendan Sorsby. At around 6'4" and 255 pounds, Royer sports a dense and compact build, with very good proportions. Though his length runs on the shorter side proportionally, slightly impacting his catch radius, he's a very sure-handed target, who also has the separation framework to create space on his own. He's a fleet-footed and fluid mover leading into stems, he has the hip flexibility to sink and hinge around efficiently, and his mobility profile supports a full route tree. As a blocker, Royer's want-to is present, at the very least. He needs to improve his leveraging and power sustain at the NFL level, as that can hurt his utility in-line. That said, he has immediate rotational appeal as a potential pass-game asset with serviceable blocking value, and quality starter upside is present.

name: Zane Durant
blurb: Zane Durant was on a steep upward trajectory after a three-sack, 11-TFL 2024 campaign, but his stock stagnated a bit in a 2025 campaign where Durant converted a career-high four sacks, but saw his TFL production drastically decrease. He now stands as a high-upside but polarizing mid-round prospect at DT. Measuring in at 6'1", 290 pounds, with arms over 32" and 10' mitts for hands, Durant has a picture-perfect combination of natural leverage and proportional length, and he combines that with elite first-step and gap-splicing explosion off the snap. He's a wicked penetrator in the run game who can ID blocking concepts and get skinny through gaps, but he also has impressive lower-body power for his size, which he can channel with his natural leverage to stack and slab blocks 1-on-1. Meanwhile, in the pass-rush phase, Durant can use his burst, lateral agility, and well-leveraged power profile to puncture solo blocks and compress pocket space for opposing QBs. Physically, Durant is tailor-made to be an odd-front disruptor in one-gapping schemes, but his pass-rush plan and counter arsenal needs more refinement, and he sometimes sacrifices block integrity when attempting to shoot gaps as a run defender. Right away, he can serve as a dynamic rotational presence at and around 3-tech. Whether he rises beyond that projection depends on his ongoing development.

name: Nicholas Singleton
blurb: Nicholas Singleton is a gifted playmaker who's been long anticipated as a 2026 NFL Draft prospect. The 6'0", 226-pound RB, who totaled almost 4,500 yards from scrimmage and 54 scores across his four-year career, has been clocked at 23.6 MPH, and is rumored to run the 40-yard dash at 4.35 seconds. It isn't surprising that, on film, Singleton's vertical explosion and seam-stretching speed is one of his best traits, and he can use that explosive element to create chunk plays both on outside runs and designed targets. Singleton's vision, creative instincts, and hip flexibility are all suspect, but he's a versatile receiving back with a clear usage plan in the ground game: Get him in space on more linear wide-zone concepts and let his speed and forward-churning physicality take over. As a creator, Singleton's lacking vision and hip flexibility limit his RB1 upside, but he's worthy of a spot in an RB rotation as a north-south sparkplug and designed screen threat.

name: De'Zhaun Stribling
blurb: De'Zhaun Stribling is a Day 3 WR prospect with an intriguing blend of size, athleticism, and every-down utility. Originally a three-star recruit from Hawaii, Stribling began his career with the Washington State Cougars and broke out as a true freshman. Across his first two collegiate seasons, he totaled 95 catches for 1,073 yards and 10 touchdowns, then transferred to Oklahoma State. He missed all but four games to a hand injury in his first season with the Cowboys, then went 52-882-6 in 2024, before closing out his career with a 55-811-6 campaign at Ole Miss. Along the way, Stribling earned a solid 78 PFSN WR Impact score, and the analytics paint an even better picture. Per TruMedia, Stribling boasted a meager 2% drop rate with the Rebels, as well as a near-10% catch rate over expectation, and 2.29 RAC yards over expectation per catch. Stribling produced from the get-go in college, and that early breakout tracks with his high-level tools. At 6'2", 207 pounds, with near-32" arms and sub-4.4 speed, Stribling has a tantalizing size-speed profile on the vertical plane, as well as the combined catch radius and body control to make impressive high-difficulty catches at multiple ranges. Stribling's route tree needs more development, but his non-elite hip fluidity and bend slightly reduces his ceiling in that department, and that could confine him to a rotational role at his maximum. Having said all this, Stribling has clear utility as a combined vertical and RAC threat with his speed, vision, physicality, and catch-point conversion, and he's an excellent blocker as well. While he may never be a full-time starter, Stribling has a high-floor as a quality WR3 and rotational presence with niche receiving value and multi-phase utility.

name: Jaishawn Barham
blurb: Jaishawn Barham was a heavily anticipated transfer addition for the Michigan Wolverines in 2024, after accruing 10 TFLs and seven sacks in his first two seasons at Maryland. Barham's first season at Michigan wasn't as fruitful as many hoped. He met expectations with his physicality at 6'3", 248 pounds, but proved hot-and-cold both with his instincts and comfort, both in gap patrol and in coverage. In 2025, he was utilized in a role closer to that of a full-time EDGE defender and flashed promise in that role, earning double-digit TFLs and four sacks. Once again, situational awareness and discipline proved to be concerns at times, but Barham's hyper-elite explosion, reactive athleticism, ankle flexibility, and speed-to-power capacity invite excitement for his long-term projection as a pass-rusher. Barham is an explosive, supercharged athlete at his size with awe-inspiring power and pop at contact, which he can use to control blocks and stack-and-shed. He's still very raw as a pass-rush operator and lacks a fully fleshed out counter arsenal, and is largely reliant on his physical tools as a result. It may take a year or two for him to ramp up his development, but in the right environment, Barham has the raw tools and the unhinged physical temperament to become a dynamic pass-rush catalyst in odd and hybrid-front schemes, attacking downhill from wider alignments.

name: Keagen Trost
blurb: Keagen Trost is a seventh-year senior who will be a 25-year old rookie after a collegiate career that spanned across four schools and featured 42 starts, all at tackle. Trost capped off his career at Missouri in 2025, where he produced the highest individual PFSN individual OL Impact grade in the entire FBS, with a score of 99.9. Working against SEC competition, Trost allowed a measly 1.7% true pressure rate, per TruMedia, and he distinguished himself as a consistent, assignment-sound run blocker as well. At around 6'5", 311 pounds, with sub-33" arms, Trost will receive looks as a tackle-to-guard convert, and at the very least, he profiles as a position-diverse depth piece with an impressively sound foundation. He's a good athlete with functional quickness, flexibility, and range, and while he's not overly powerful, he can at least drive lower-body power with leg churn and contend with his latch and anchor strength. In pass protection, he's incredibly natural at playing square to rushers with sharp upper-lower sync and knee bend, and he picks up stunts with ease. In the run game, he's unflinchingly assignment-sound, angle-sound, and has a finisher's edge in space. And in both phases, he adheres to consistent leveraging, base load, and pad level maintenance. He's older, with raw power capacity limitations, but Trost has clear mid-level starter utility and could be an excellent mid-round value.

name: Fa'alili Fa'amoe
blurb: Fa'alili Fa'amoe followed Washington State head coach Jake Dickert to Wake Forest, for what was his sixth and final season at the CFB level. Though he's an older prospect with predominantly right tackle experience, Fa'amoe has the physical and intangible traits to challenge for starting reps early in his NFL career, likely as a tackle-to-guard convert. At 6'5", 314 pounds, with 34" arms, Fa'amoe has stellar lean mass and proportional length, and he's a light-footed lateral mover who excels at staying square with rushers and keeping balance. He uses smooth footwork and active hands to corral attackers on passing downs, and he has clear people-moving ability in the run game with his linear explosiveness, driving power, and relentless physicality. Fa'amoe is a bit stiff in the hips, which reduces his recovery range; that's been the main driver for talks of a transition to guard. But his experience and upper-lower sync aids him at tackle, and should he shift inside eventually, he has the phone-booth profile and physicality to be a quality player.

name: Taurean York
blurb: Taurean York joined the Texas A&M Aggies as a three-star recruit in 2023, and immediately emerged as a full-time starter. In 2024, he was named a team captain as a true sophomore, and in 2025, he functioned as the team's "green dot" LB, calling signals pre-snap and getting teammates into position. At 5'10", with likely sub-31" arms, Taurean York won't be every defensive coordinator's preferred mold. But for those who can look past the size, York stands out as one of the best MIKE linebackers in the class. He's an elite processor who can command his unit pre-snap and toy with offensive protection looks to get favorable matchups, and he's a fast-flowing support presence post-snap, who can erase running space with his lightning-quick burst and closing speed. On top of it all, he's a fluid and instinctive coverage LB. As one might expect, York's tackling can be inconsistent at times with his lacking length, but he has good take-on, angle IQ, and form overall, and his impact as a central second-level presence arguably outweighs any volatility at contact. While his size will always be a slight limitation, York has the range and mental proficiency to supersede it and thrive as an NFL starter.

name: Jordan Hudson
blurb: Jordan Hudson is a compelling sleeper in the 2026 class. A former four-star recruit who played his first season at TCU, Hudson transferred to SMU in 2023 and immediately produced as a go-to red-zone threat, with seven touchdowns on just 30 catches. Hudson progressed into a primary target in 2025, amassing 61 catches for 766 yards and six scores. That production is solid if unspectacular on the surface, but his advanced numbers and efficiency metrics are glowing, particularly in an SMU offense that often went off-schedule. Hudson's 79.7 PFSN WR Impact score is strong, and per TruMedia, he boasted 2.35 yards per route run, a meager 3.7% drop rate, a 6.56% catch rate over expectation, and almost seven yards of RAC per attempt. On film, Hudson intrigues further. At 6'1", 193 pounds, he's close to average size, with good-not-great explosion and only above-average speed. Nevertheless, he's more quick than fast, and boasts excellent short-area agility and twitch, elite lateral explosive range and hip sink, and the retraction freedom and bend to excel as a route runner 1-on-1. And at the catch, he's one of the best in the class with his elite body control, flexibility, and high-end reactive quickness. He needs to bring more consistent urgency as a blocker, but Hudson has clear WR2/3 appeal.

name: Nick Barrett
blurb: Nick Barrett is a mid-to-late-round nose tackle prospect who stands as one of the more intriguing value acquisitions in the 2026 NFL Draft. In the transfer portal era, Barrett was the rare three-star Power conference recruit who stayed with his original school for his entire five-year career. Barrett was primarily a special teams and depth player from 2021 to 2023, and missed all but five games in 2024 due to injury. But in 2025, Barrett embarked on a late-career breakout, amassing 42 tackles, six tackles for loss, two sacks, and a fumble recovery touchdown. Along the way, he achieved a solid 77.4 PFSN DT Impact grade, and allowed just 1.86 yards per run stop, per TruMedia. At 6'3", 312 pounds, with 33 3/8" arms and 10" hands, Barrett is an imposing, thickly-built interior defender with high hips and modest hip stiffness, but also the combined linear explosion and heavy hands to present some measured utility as a pass-rushing threat. He has the elite motor and the functional first-step to provide measured juice as a pass-rusher, but his prime function in an NFL rotation will be as a tree stump on early downs, with the explosive hands and combative disposition to control interior gaps early and muddy opposing looks. With his violent extension ability and elite stacking strength, he can slab, control, and shed solo blocks as a one-gapper, and his thick, powerful lower-body enables him to reset the point, acquire leverage, and root against double-teams and combo blocks. Resembling a more explosive Derrick Nnadi, Barrett has the tools to be a quality rotational presence with clear value at the fulcrum.

name: Jake Slaughter
blurb: Jake Slaughter started 33 games at center, playing for Florida in the SEC gauntlet for his entire career. In 2025, he achieved the third-highest PFSN OL Impact score in the entire nation, with an elite grade of 93.3, while allowing just four pressures. The metrics speak heavily in favor of Slaughter, but his professional projection is a bit more complicated. At a lean 303 pounds, Slaughter lacks high-end mass or length, and he doesn't have high-end explosiveness to compensate either; all of this visibly detracts from his maximum power capacity at contact, particulary in the run game. Slaughter is intelligent and assignment-sound, but isn't a natural-born displacer, and he shines more as a pure pass protector. In the pass blocking phase, Slaughter's fluid and light-footed lateral athleticism enables him to match and stay square to threats. He can gather rushers with precise, active hands, flexible leveraging, and upper-lower sync. And pre-snap, he has experience calling out blitz threats and commanding the front. Slaughter will best fit pass-heavy offenses, but regardless, he has the potential to be a quality NFL starter if he can withstand NFL power and siphon out more of his own.

name: Michael Taaffe
blurb: Michael Taaffe is a potential mid-round gem in the 2026 NFL Draft safety class, whose career is a testament to the merit of perseverance. He initially committed to Rice out of high school, but later made the decision to walk-on at Texas. He redshirted his first season, but saw more snaps and earned a scholarship as a redshirt freshman. In 2023, Taaffe emerged as a regular defensive contributor, and in 2024, he earned All-American honors after logging 78 tackles, 5.5 tackles for loss, two sacks, two interceptions, and 10 pass breakups. Taaffe's raw production in 2025 wasn't quite as eye-catching, but he still registered a strong PFSN Safety Impact grade of 85, and followed that up with a solid Senior Bowl showing. At around 6'0", 195 pounds, with sub-30" arms, Taaffe is close to average size overall. This affects his ability to get off blocks, and he doesn't have elite explosiveness or long speed. Taaffe compensates, however, with the short-area quickness and fluidity one would expect from a former cornerback, and he's a heady, intelligent defender with the spatial awareness, processing ability, and angle IQ to play sound football from single-high, two-high, and roving positions in the box. Couple this with a meager 3% missed tackle rate in 2025 per TruMedia, and Taaffe stands out as a exceedingly well-rounded, if unspectacular, safety prospect, with quality starter potential.

name: Taylen Green
blurb: Taylen Green stands to benefit as one of the more traits-rich quarterbacks in the sparse 2026 NFL Draft class. The Mountain West Freshman of the Year back in 2022, Green took his talents to Arkansas and failed to show substantial year-over-year development, but nonetheless managed to enthrall with flashes of elite physical talent and 1-on-1 discernment. At his best, Green showed he could layer the ball over second-level defenders with pace and touch, operate off-platform on designed rollouts, and generate eye-catching velocity from different launch points. However, Green's field vision, accuracy, and risk aversion remain sore areas, even after four years as a starter. Green can catapult up boards with a strong pre-draft cycle, and has the raw physical talent of a starter, but it remains to be seen if he can handle the hastened pace of the NFL. In the right environment, with time to sit, he could grow, but his more likely outcome is that of a backup and eventual spot-starter.

name: Sam Roush
blurb: Sam Roush joined the Stanford Cardinal in 2022 and saw immediate action as a rotational blocker and special teams contributor. In 2023, he emerged as a full-time starter as a true sophomore, and he caught 40 passes as a junior in 2024. Roush's senior campaign was his most productive; he accrued 49 catches for 545 yards and two touchdowns, earning a strong PFSN TE Impact grade of 83.9. The raw talent has always been clear with Roush, and that's part of what allowed him to acclimate so quickly at the collegiate level. At around 6'5", 260 pounds, Roush has great size and explosiveness, to go along with steely competitive toughness and physicality. He's an excellent blocking presence who, though he experiences lapses in balance and technical control, has the range, corrective quickness, and power profile to position himself effectively and drive through blocks. Meanwhile, as a pass-catcher, his production is a function of his smooth seam-splitting athleticism and box-out ability. Roush's catching technique can be tighter and more consistent, and his stem work as a route runner still needs major work, but he passes the requisite athletic and intangible thresholds, and his efficiency as a producer bodes well for his development as a potential high-end TE2 and eventual starter down the line.

name: Daylen Everette
blurb: Daylen Everette joined Georgia as a five-star recruit back in 2022, and went on to serve as a three-year starter in Kirby Smart's system. Everette's first season as a starter was up-and-down, but his potential nonetheless popped off the screen, and his 2024 campaign was a step in the right direction. In 2025, he achieved a career-high 10 pass deflections, while earning a solid PFSN CB Impact grade of 81.8. At 6'1", 196 pounds, with near-32" arms, Everette boasts elite quantifiable athleticism, as evidenced by his 4.38 40-yard dash and 37.5" vertical. With gliding long-strider speed, Everette appears most comfortable in side-saddle and trail, where he can position himself to wall off opponents and contend for targets. He flourished as a playmaker and turnover generator with three INTs in 2024, and flashed that same potential in 2025, but pressing issues from earlier in his career still remain on his scouting report. While Everette is a venerable vertical athlete, he at times struggles to sink his hips and swiftly redirect overtop comeback routes and hitches, and can experience delays in plant-and-drive efficiency as a result. His reaction speed and 2-on-1 route instincts can be sharper as well, and his playmaking precision working with his back to the ball still needs work. Everette's NFL-ready frame and physicality ingrains his utility in run support, and in zone-heavy schemes, he has starter upside, but he needs to grow more consistent on route response and spatial management before ascending past a rotational role.

name: Keyron Crawford
blurb: Keyron Crawford stands out as an ideal value in the "designated pass rusher" mold. He began his career at Arkansas State in 2022, and in 2023, he emerged as a high-quality pressure producer, amassing 5.5 sacks and 10.5 tackles for loss. In 2024, he transferred to Auburn, and though he spent his first year with the Tigers behind Keldric Faulk and Jalen McLeod on the depth chart, he took up a place opposite Faulk as a quality starter in 2025. In his final collegiate season, Crawford accumulated five sacks, 9.5 TFLs, and a strong PFSN EDGE Impact grade of 84.2. Per TruMedia, he also logged an impressive pressure rate of almost 20%. At 6'4", 251 pounds, Crawford has good frame density, and though his arms are shorter than average, he compensates for his lacking power profile with sufficient burst, bend, agility, leveraging skills, and pass-rush nuance, and he brings urgency in pursuit. He may never have the sheer size or play strength to play with his hand in the dirt in even-front schemes consistently, but as an odd and hybrid-front rush linebacker and designated pass rusher, he has immediate rotational appeal, and could go on to become a mid-level starting talent.

name: Markel Bell
blurb: Markel Bell is a mammoth tackle who'll be a prime middle-round target for teams that value overwhelming size, power, and strength. Bell began his career at Holmes Community College after a late growth spurt in high school, and was an NJCAA all-region performer in 2023. As the top JUCO prospect in 2024, Bell signed with the Miami Hurricanes, and started five of 12 games played in his first season. In 2025, he was the Hurricanes' full-time starter at left tackle opposite Francis Mauigoa. Bell earned a Senior Bowl invite for his play, and at times struggled against explosive talents with superior leverage, but he improved over the course of the week and remains a compelling scheme-specific talent. At 6'9", 346 pounds, with around 36 1/2" arms and a 7'3" wingspan, Bell is one of the biggest players to hit the draft circuit in league history. That ultra-rare size comes with some natural trade-offs. Bell is a good athlete for his size, but is naturally a bit lumbering and slow in recovery. He's not the most flexible in his hips either, and playing with proper leverage and pad level is a struggle, to say the least. Bell counteracts that need for superior leverage to a degree with his careful footwork, angle IQ, and ability to swivel back his anchor and dominate opponents with his size alone, but he'll need to keep refining his hand usage and timing at the NFL level. Bell has low-level starting potential in gap and inside zone schemes, but may function best as a swing OT.

name: Cyrus Allen
blurb: Cyrus Allen is a potential mid-round sleeper in the 2026 NFL Draft WR class, who was one of the prime standouts at the Senior Bowl. He began his career as a three-star signee at Louisiana Tech in 2022, and immediately established himself as a dynamic deep threat, with 22 catches for 500 yards and four touchdowns as a true freshman. After another year of production at LTU, Allen transferred to Texas A&M, but struggled to catch on with the Aggies, and ultimately transferred to Cincinnati for his final season. He waged a career-best campaign with the Bearcats, racking up 51 catches for 674 yards and 13 scores, while earning an 80.3 PFSN WR Impact grade. Per TruMedia, he also achieved a strong 2.6 yards per route run, a size-defying 12.23% catch rate over expectation, and a miniscule 1.5% drop rate. At 5'11", 190 pounds, with just over 31" arms, Allen is average size at best, but he possesses a dynamic brand of mobility, complete with electrified agility, twitch, stop-and-start, and propulsive vertical explosion and speed. He's more apt as a spatial manipulator than a contact deconstructor when running routes, and will need to prove he can attune to NFL physicality against press and at stems. But for teams that value speed, quickness, and natural separation at WR, Allen has all of the desirable tools, to pair with plus body control and proactive hands. Right away, Allen can be a quality rotational sparkplug with deep threat appeal, and he has quality WR2/3 upside.

name: Roman Hemby
blurb: Roman Hemby arrived at Indiana after four years at Maryland, where he never saw more than 190 carries in a season but built a reputation as one of college football’s most dependable all-purpose backs. At Indiana, he eclipsed 1,000 yards for the first and only time in his career, all while maintaining his trademark efficiency. At 6'0", 208 pounds, Hemby pairs a sturdy, compact frame with outstanding vision and burst, and he's a quality elusive threat with his crisp footwork and smooth lateral mobility. With over 100 career catches and just three drops, he boasts some of the surest hands out of the backfield as a bonus. Hemby doesn't have domineering physicality or strength, nor is he particularly effective in pass protection, but Hemby’s play speed, patience, and one-cut quickness make him a natural fit in zone schemes. Projecting forward, his skill set profiles best as a versatile rotational back or pass-game specialist.

name: Max Llewellyn
blurb: Max Llewellyn was one of the more productive pass-rushers at the collegiate level over the past two seasons, and looms large as a mid-round sleeper in the 2026 NFL Draft EDGE class. Llewellyn redshirted at Iowa in 2021 and played sparingly in 2022, but became a consistent contributor in 2023 and was a full-time starter in 2024, winning the team's Hustle Award. Llewellyn ended his time at Iowa with a career-best 2025 season, earning 6.5 sacks, 9.5 tackles for loss, six pass deflections, and a solid PFSN EDGE Impact grade of 79.7. Per TruMedia, he also logged a strong 12.5% true pressure rate, good for Top 15 in the entire 2026 class. At 6'5", 255 pounds, with 32 5/8" arms, Llewellyn has a somewhat unorthodox build. He's tall and low-cut, with good lean mass, but is much lighter in his lower body, and is relatively short-armed. He's best as a pass-rusher, where he can use his lateral agility and fluidity to win with wicked spins and swipe combos, as well as bend around the arc. However, he might not be explosive enough to hang his hat on that in the NFL, nor does he have the raw power element to supplement his finesse. Additionally, while he brings great gap intelligence, leverage-attuning flexibility, and motor in run defense, his middling play strength renders him easy to move at times. Llewellyn may ultimately max out as a quality rotational presence with primary pass-rush appeal, but he has low-level starting potential at his peak, as well as measured schematic flexibility.

name: DeMonte Capehart
blurb: DeMonte Capehart spent six seasons with the Clemson Tigers, and was never a full-time starter through an entire season. His production somewhat reflects that, but Capehart's valuable role sometimes goes overlooked as a result. Capehart won't have the acclaim of former first-round picks from the school's ranks, or anticipated top prospect Peter Woods, but he nonetheless presents his own kind of appeal as a future NFL player. At 6'5", 313 pounds, with near-34" arms, Capehart is a long-levered gap occupier with the strength and dislodge resistance of a tree trunk. His hyper-elite explosiveness aids his ability to fully reset the point-of-attack and pose a grave threat for offensive linemen attempting displacement. At his size, Capehart boasts a blistering 4.85 40-yard dash with a 1.71 10-yard split, as well as a logic-defying 33.5" vertical. He makes his money in the run game, surging into contact with unmatched burst and force output, controlling blocks, one-gapping from as far inside as 0-tech and 1-tech, and puncturing holes in zone looks with his volcanic gap penetrating speed. And while his pass-rush arsenal is very rudimentary, his rare linear burst and raw power produce a fearsome bull-rush on passing downs. A true "dirty work" DT with the superlative physical tools to exist safely against NFL competition, Capehart's two-phase ceiling is unclear, but his floor as an uber-explosive, early-down one-gapping pest and gap occluder at interior alignments is readily apparent.

name: Kendal Daniels
blurb: Kendal Daniels is one of the most intriguing projected Day 3 prospects in the 2026 NFL Draft. Originally a four-star and Top 50 safety recruit, Daniels played his first four seasons at Oklahoma State, earning Big 12 Defensive Freshman of the Year honors in 2022. After a 2024 campaign that saw him amass 10.5 tackles for loss, 5.5 sacks, and seven pass breakups, he transferred up to the Oklahoma Sooners in the SEC, and played a hybrid nickel and dime linebacker role in Brent Venables' scheme. In his lone season in Norman, he registered 53 tackles, nine TFLs, and three PBUs, earning an invite to the Senior Bowl. At 6'5", 242 pounds, Daniels has near-elite size at LB, and yet he moves with rare quickness and fluidity. He's extremely nuanced in off-man and zone coverage, with easy sink and redirection for his size, and while he doesn't have high-end long speed, he has the explosiveness and half-field range to close downhill, erase gaps in half-field pursuit, and drape middle-field receiving threats. Daniels' tackling needs work; he's very inconsistent with his form and his breakdown angles. That said, Daniels is a superb coverage presence with alignment versatility, he's a relentless contact engager in the box with quick-firing reaction, extremely willing physicality, and elite power capacity when stacking blocks, and he flashes pass-rush upside in spurts. His player archetype is a unique one that will demand the proper fit, but Daniels has the potential to be a quality role player and eventual quality starter, with projected special teams value.

name: Dae'Quan Wright
blurb: Dae'Quan Wright is a high-upside Day 3 TE prospect in the 2026 NFL Draft. He originally suited up for Virginia Tech as a four-star signee in 2022 and 2023, compiling 47 catches and 574 yards across two seasons with the Hokies. In 2024, Wright transferred to Ole Miss to play under Lane Kiffin. The result was two consecutive career-best seasons, culminating in a 2025 campaign that saw Wright log 39 catches for 635 yards and five TDs, as well as a solid PFSN TE Impact grade of 80.6. In particular, Wright excelled as a RAC threat, achieving a RAC yards over expectation figure of 6.16 that ranks first place among TE prospects in draftable range. At 6'4" and almost 250 pounds with over 32" arms, Wright has good size and excellent linear athleticism. He's explosive in space with powerful long-strider acceleration and effortless long speed, and his combined strength and speed make him a bear to contend with for solo tacklers in the RAC phase. While his route running execution can oscillate between methodical and lethargic, he flashes good stem IQ, cutting flexibility on breaks, and zone awareness, he has strong hands in contact situations, and he's a powerful, physical presence as a blocker, with the capability to play in-line, insert into gaps from H-back alignments, or block out in space and drive into opponents with energetic leg churn. Wright's over-arching consistency remains a point of emphasis. He can be prone to mental blunders at times, and while his hands are decent, he can improve at controlling the catch point in contested situations with timing and instincts. Nevertheless, Wright has immediate appeal as a high-upside TE2 with RAC and blocking value, and he has the upside to be an impact starter in time, with more development.

name: Thaddeus Dixon
blurb: Thaddeus Dixon is a high-upside cornerback prospect in the mid-to-late rounds of the 2026 NFL Draft. After racking up five interceptions in two seasons at Long Beach City College through 2021 and 2022, he transferred to Washington as a prized JUCO recruit, and immediately took on a starting role. His 2024 season with the Huskies was his best career year; he amassed an INT, two tackles for loss, and 10 pass breakups, while earning a strong PFSN CB Impact grade of 84. In 2025, his PFSN CB Impact grade fell to 77.1, but he still flashed promise on an embattled North Carolina squad. At the Senior Bowl, Dixon recaptured some of that 2024 magic. Particularly later in the week, he was one of the best CBs present, showing off sticky press-man ability, keen instincts in zone, and a playmaking zeal that set him apart. At around 6'0", 195 pounds, with 31 1/2" arms, Dixon has decent length, to pair with springy explosive athleticism, elite reactive coil and twitch, unhinged physical energy, and great hip sink and malleability on breaks. Dixon's experience has bestowed him with stellar technical variability, processing speed, and run support chops, and while he doesn't have high-end vertical speed, his long-strider explosion aids in recovery. Dixon was pulled over multiple times for speeding in his time at Chapel Hill, and also received a citation for reckless driving. Thus, teams will have to thoroughly vet his off-field discipline. But if Dixon passes the character evaluation, he has compelling traits as an immediate rotational contributor and potential scheme-diverse NFL starter down the road.

name: Jaeden Roberts
blurb: Jaeden Roberts only spent one season as a full-time starter for the Alabama Crimson Tide, but his physical framework should nonetheless earn mid-to-late round interest from NFL evaluators in April. At 6'5" and 327 pounds, Roberts' length and imposing mass are central to his game, and his physicality stands out routinely on tape. In the run game, Roberts can use his superlative length to strike and fully extend against gap defenders, and he's also able to pull to the C-gap and slab crashing ends. Meanwhile, in pass protection, his wide base and violent punches lend well to early advantages at contact. Balance through engagement is still a major issue for Roberts, who can be slow and lumbering with his recovery footwork and inconsistent with his pad level management, and his stunt vision can improve as well. However, in spite of his limitations with recovery athleticism and flexibility, Roberts has the size, power, and physicality to hold up in a phone booth, and seal off corridors in gap schemes. He projects as solid interior depth right away, and could take notable reps as a replacement-level starter.

name: Eli Heidenreich
blurb: Eli Heidenreich is an exciting hybrid prospect with both RB and WR capabilities. He spent his entire collegiate career at Navy, first riding the bench in 2022 before emerging as a dynamic gadget threat in 2023. Heidenreich's best season came in 2025; he ran for 499 yards and three touchdowns on 77 carries, while also hauling in 941 yards and six TDs on 51 catches. But Heidenreich's three-year production is just as impressive. Over that stretch, he amassed a combined 3,151 yards from scrimmage and 23 total scores, averaging almost seven yards per carry and over 18 yards per catch. Strictly at RB in 2025, Heidenreich boasted a strong PFSN Impact score of 80.5, but he also achieved a mind-boggling 4.97 yards per route run, per TruMedia. At 6'0" and almost 200 pounds, with mid-4.4 speed, near-elite explosiveness numbers, and a blistering 6.55 three-cone, Heidenreich passes the desired athletic threshold with flying colors, and his film is an absolute joy. As a runner, Heidenreich's speed, burst, agility, and processing in space take center stage, while as a receiver, he shows off a surprisingly vast route tree and alignment pallet, high-level stem IQ and nuance, electric RAC ability, and competent hands. Heidenreich functions better in space than he does when attempting to process and navigate within congested looks, and he's not quite as proficient working through contact as desired. Heidenreich's deference for spatial operation suggests his more favorable future exists as a gadget WR who can be schemed touches off motions, and not as a volume runner. Having said this, Heidenreich's usage versatility is a boon unique to him, and as a bonus, he's an extremely willing, physical blocker in space. For the team that knows how to use him, Heidenreich can be an incredibly valuable rotational catalyst and do-it-all weapon.

name: Delby Lemieux
blurb: Delby Lemieux is an intriguing mid-round offensive line prospect with college experience at tackle, but projected versatility to center and guard. Lemieux played his entire career at Darmouth as a tackle; he served as a full-time starter from 2023 through 2025, earning first-team All-Ivy honors in 2024 and 2025. Lemieux's performance was strong enough to earn him an invite to the Senior Bowl, where he took reps at center and distinguished himself as an offseason riser. Later, competing at Boston College's pro day, Lemieux registered a 5.02 40-yard dash, a 7.52 three-cone, and a 1.71 10-yard split that places in the 97th percentile among offensive linemen. Lemieux's testing confirms what's evident on film: He's an elite athlete at the position, with high-level explosiveness, range in space, and corrective quickness when recalibrating his base. Just as important, however, is Lemieux's flexibility. Despite being just 305 pounds, his torso flexibility and free anchor swivel aids in power absorption and leveraging, his hip fluidity enables him to adjust his base alignment and transition freely on recovery, and he's ultra-consistent at retaining leg action and using effervescent leg churn to maximize his lower-body power output and drive in the run game. There are some concerns as Lemieux makes the NFL leap. He may need a slight acclimation period coming from the FCS, and the move to center will require a reorientation for processing complex blitz and stunt looks. Additionally, his subpar arm length does affect his latch strength at times, allowing longer opponents to decouple. In the immediate timeline, Lemieux might serve better as athletic, position-versatile depth, but he has true impact starter potential as a center or guard in zone-heavy schemes with his athleticism, flexibility, angle IQ, leverage profile, and physicality.

name: VJ Payne
blurb: VJ Payne is a high-upside safety prospect in the 2026 NFL Draft, but has questions regarding his ultimate role at the NFL level. Payne joined the Wildcats as a three-star linebacker recruit in 2022, and started the first game of his true freshman season at free safety. He'd start three more games that year, and from 2023 through 2025, he started 38 consecutive contests. In 2025, Payne had arguably his best career season. He earned a strong PFSN Safety Impact score of 86, and per TruMedia, he allowed just 0.4 yards per coverage snap, and missed just 5.3% of his tackle attempts. There's a lot of cosmetic appeal to Payne's profile. He's a three-year starter and team captain. He's a lab-built size-speed athlete at 6'3", 206 pounds, with near-34" arms and 4.4 speed. The conundrum with Payne is this: He has the size, athleticism, and tackling ability to be an asset in run support, but doesn't quite have the urgency, angle consistency, or enforcer edge to control blocks and take his game over the top. And in coverage, while he has the combined size, explosion, range, corrective twitch, and fluidity to be an alignment-diverse force, his ball skills are decidely below-average, and his plant-and-drive footwork can still improve. Payne has two-high, big-nickel, and strong safety versatility right out of the gate, and should have high rotational and spot-starter value early on. Whether or not he reaches his impact starter ceiling depends on whether or not he further refines his coverage game and adopts the targeted tenacity required to run the table in NFL run defense.

name: Drew Allar
blurb: Drew Allar is perhaps the most quintessential "prototypical QB" in the 2026 NFL Draft. A former five-star recruit, he has the frame at 6'5", 235 pounds. He has the rocket arm, and he has the nimble mobility and arm elasticity to work off-platform. He also accrued a glowing record of 26-9 in his time as a starter, and helped lead Penn State to the CFB Playoff semifinals in 2024, showing glimpses of very real pre-snap command, post-snap processing, and break anticipation in the process. While Allar has an enticing blend of raw talent and offensive command, however, flaws remain on his profile. His accuracy and precision runs very hot-and-cold, his dropback and release mechanics are near liability level, and he's not the level of rushing threat to create his way out of bad situations consistently. His season-ending ankle injury suffered in November also clouds his immediate availability. Allar will check all the cosmetic boxes for teams, and he'll be just a 22-year old rookie despite being a three-year collegiate starter. He's a low-price, low-risk investment in later rounds, but his mechanics essentially need to be rebuilt from the ground up before he can take live reps reliably.

name: Tyler Onyedim
blurb: Tyler Onyedim is a traits-rich Day 3 DT prospect with exciting long-term potential. Onyedim played the first four seasons of his career at Iowa State, redshirting the 2021 campaign after signing as a three-star recruit. From 2022 to 2024, he gained a more prevalent role in the Cyclones' rotation, totaling 86 tackles, three sacks, and 12 tackles for loss across that three-year stretch. Onyedim played his final collegiate season at Texas A&M, and comfortably had a career-best year. Playing in 13 games, he amassed 48 tackles, 8.5 TFLs, and 2.5 sacks, while earning a solid PFSN DT Impact score of 81.9. Per TruMedia, he logged an impressive 4.7% TFL percentage in run defense. That's where Onyedim thrives right now: As an obstructive and disruptive force on early downs. At 6'3 1/2" and 292 pounds, with over 34" arms, as well as elite explosive athleticism, Onyedim has a picture-perfect blend of natural leverage, compact mass, length, and burst off the line. He can use his leverage and length to stack blocks and keep point-of-contact authority, and his elite explosion and penetrating speed can make him a menacing TFL and pursuit threat as a one-gap invader. As a pass-rusher, Onyedim still remains a work-in-progress after four years of extensive on-field experience. He flashes excellent power output and has an impressive spin move at his size, but his pass-rush arsenal is still below-average overall. Inconsistent timing and precision can affect his force output, his upper and lower body aren't always in-sync, and his lacking counter arsenal can make him easy to gather if his initial moves don't land. Onyedim is largely a developmental prospect, but he has a respectable run defense floor and immediate alignment versatility between 3-tech and 5-tech. Right away, he can serve as rotational depth. A mid-level starting career is most likely down the road, but he has impact starter potential if he can flesh out his pass-rush skills.

name: Nadame Tucker
blurb: Nadame Tucker is an incredibly intriguing Day 3 pass-rush catalyst in the 2026 NFL Draft. He'll be a 26-year old rookie, and thus may fall outside certain teams' age thresholds, and his size also complicates matters. He's around 6'2", 246 pounds, with just 32" arms. Tucker's "sweet spot" for interested NFL teams may be narrow, but he also has the non-negotiable qualities that teams crave: Athleticism, motor, and love for the game. Tucker didn't play football until his senior year in high school, and was forced to take the JUCO route. Dominant production at Hutchinson CC led him to Houston, and eventually Western Michigan, where he turned in an incredible 2025 campaign. In 2025, Tucker led the FBS with 14.5 sacks and 21 tackles for loss. Per TruMedia, his true pressure percentage of 17.9% was second-best in the FBS only to David Bailey, and his 23.8% pressure conversion was well above the 90th percentile. In odd and hybrid-front schemes, Tucker can make an instant impact as a designated pass-rusher with his explosive, energized athleticism, electrified power and knockback profile, red-hot motor, and his working pass-rush arsenal and understanding of upper-lower synergy. He'll be 30 years old by the end of his first contract, but within that window, he can bring an immediate boost to a rush rotation, and contribute with consistency.

name: Keyshaun Elliott
blurb: Keyshaun Elliott is an intriguing mid-round LB prospect with a recognizable starter-level floor and exciting upside if he can keep building his frame. Elliott began his career as an unheralded signee with the New Mexico State Aggies in 2022, and immediately earned rotational reps as a true freshman. In 2023, Elliott was an emergent All-CUSA performer, and transferred to Arizona State as a junior in 2024. In 2024, he retained his impact role, but the 2025 campaign saw Elliott produce career-best numbers in several important categories. Starting 12 games for the Sun Devils, Elliott amassed 98 tackles, 14 tackles for loss, and seven sacks. Per TruMedia, he achieved an incredible 68.2% pressure rate on blitzing reps. Elliott's most prevalent value comes as a run defender and blitz component. At around 6'2", 231 pounds, with 4.58 speed, he's well-built and rangy tracking plays laterally, and he's one of the best natural processors in the class, with elite processing, vision, and reaction-to-stimulus when encountering angle fluctuations. There are elements of Elliott's game that still demand improvement. An 18% missed tackle rate from 2025 is extremely concerning, and points to a length limitation as well as issues breaking down and maintaining tackling angles in pursuit. Additionally, while Elliott has good coverage mobility and zone feel, his ball tracking and blind spot IQ leaves more to be desired. At 231 pounds, adding more mass to his frame will also be imperative. Having said all this, Elliott has the athletic and intangible qualities to be a potential mid-round gem and future quality starter at the LB position, if he can attack his areas of improvement with zeal.

name: Matthew Hibner
blurb: Matthew Hibner is a potential Day 3 gem in the 2026 NFL Draft TE class. He began his collegiate career at Michigan, where he joined as a four-star recruit. However, behind names like Colston Loveland, AJ Barner, Erick All, and Luke Schoonmaker, Hibner scarcely took meaningful reps, and transferred to SMU in 2024. Across two seasons in Dallas, he totaled 55 catches for 804 yards and eight touchdowns. In 2025, he registered a solid 78.4 PFSN TE Impact score, and his advanced analytics stoked even greater optimism. Per TruMedia, he fielded a 2.5% drop rate, allowed a mere 2.4% pressure percentage across 84 pass protection reps, and had an 8% catch rate over expectation. At 6'4", 251 pounds, with 32 3/8" arms, Hibner has decent size to pair with excellent functional athleticism. On film, he appears as a fluid and explosive mover, and his 4.57 40-yard dash and 37" vertical corroborate this. Even more beneficial for Hibner was his Senior Bowl showing. He was arguably the best TE in Mobile, showing off smooth vertical stemming ability, route running nuance, body control, and proactive hands. Hibner is a TE prospect who quietly checks almost all the boxes. He's athletic, he can threaten vertically up the seam, he can separate at multiple levels and accrue RAC, and he's a dutiful two-phase blocker with a physical edge, violent hands, good leveraging skills, and active footwork. Hibner's middling size and deconstruction against physicality as a route runner may hold him back at times, and he still has room to further refine his short and intermediate route tree. Nevertheless, Hibner profiles as a high-quality TE2 early on, and has quality starter potential.

name: Travis Burke
blurb: Travis Burke is a domineering mid-to-late-round tackle prospect in the 2026 NFL Draft, with the profile to attract allure from teams that value elite size, power, and athleticism. Burke began his career at Gardner-Webb in 2021, then transferred to Florida International for the 2023 and 2024 seasons, starting 22 games across that stretch. 2025 was Burke's lone season at Memphis, and in it, he started 11 games at right tackle, earning a strong PFSN OL Impact grade of 88 in the process. Though Burke drew 12 penalties on the year, he also allowed just a 3.9% pressure rate on true pass sets, per TruMedia. At 6'9", 325 pounds, with over 34" arms, Burke's size is purely overwhelming, and he boasts excellent size-adjusted athleticism, as evidenced by his 5.17 40-yard dash, 1.79 10-yard split, and 8'11" broad jump. On film, Burke displays the requisite initial explosion and short-area mobility to corroborate his testing, and he has enough range to pull and hunt down targets in space with his raw power. Burke's size alone is menacing, but the Tigers product also takes it upon himself to menace opponents with ruthless point-of-attack physicality, finishing torque, and mauling resolve late in reps. Burke relishes the physicality of the position, and routinely buries rushers and edge-setters when they give up leverage. He's a true tone-setter and a natural obstructor, who also flashes solid pass sets for his size, as well as solid stunt and blitz awareness. Additionally, in the run game, he's flashed the ability to execute adjacent-gap reach blocks, down blocks, as well as climb and drive through targets at the second level. As one might expect at 6'9", pad level and center-of-gravity are points of emphasis for Burke, who can align too upright and lopsided later in reps. Nevertheless, Burke has the high-end tools to field regular starting reps after a year or two of development, and he has more scheme-diversity than his frame would indicate.

name: Eric Rivers
blurb: Eric Rivers is a potential sleeper at WR in the 2026 NFL Draft, who could emerge with opportunity at the professional level. He began his collegiate career at Memphis, but didn't catch a pass through one healthy year and another impacted by injury, and transferred to Florida International in 2023. In 2024, Rivers broke out with 62 catches for 1,172 yards and 12 touchdowns, as well as a PFSN WR Impact score of 80.4 and 3.78 yards per route run. That performance earned him a chance with the Georgia Tech Yellow Jackets. While his raw production fell in Tech's run-heavy scheme, Rivers still eclipsed 650 yards, and his analytics quietly bode well. Per TruMedia, he halved his drop percentage to just 3.4% and earned a 12.99% catch rate over expectation. At around 5'10", 170 pounds, with just 30" arms, Rivers size, medical history, and age (he'll be a 24-year old rookie) could work against him. And within his archetype, he doesn't quite have elite stop-and-start, deceleration capacity, or hip sink overtop breaks. Nevertheless, Rivers has the dynamic long speed, short-area burst, and crisp lateral agility and foot speed to supplement his build. He can effectively use his speed and zone IQ to sear through creases and make himself available, he has the vertical athleticism to leap and extend for tough catches, and he can use that same weaving speed and twitch to generate RAC yardage in space. Working against immediate physicality in press could prove to be an issue for Rivers, and he'll never provide much as a blocker, but as an undersized sparkplug, he has rotational utility in an NFL WR room.

name: Robert Henry Jr.
blurb: Robert Henry Jr. was a small-school high school star who was forced to take the JUCO route early in his collegiate career. After distinguishing himself at Jones College in Mississippi, he was able to leverage his unheralded talent into a three-year opportunity with the UTSA Roadrunners. Three years later, Henry is a legitimate NFL Draft prospect, after having amassed 408 carries for 2,339 yards and 27 touchdowns in his career. In 2025 alone, Henry rushed for a career-high 1,045 yards at almost seven yards per attempt. On film, it's not hard to see why Henry has been one of the most dynamic players in the Group of Six over the past few years. At 5'9", 205 pounds, Henry is undersized, but as a mover, he's springy and explosive, with light feet, nimble lateral range, acute cutting flexibility, and the long-strider burst to take advantage of creases and cutback lanes. His natural creative athleticism as a runner is only maximized by his quick processing speed, cutback anticipation, pressing IQ, and adaptability. He'll turn 25 years old at the tail end of his rookie season, but Henry has the profile of an immediate contributor as a change-of-pace back, and he could produce even beyond that.

name: Mason Reiger
blurb: Mason Reiger is an intriguing designated pass-rusher prospect with easily visible Day 3 appeal. Reiger was originally a zero-star recruit from Conant High School in Illinois, who chose to compete as a walk-on at Louisville, rather than entertain Division II offers. He redshirted his first season in 2020, but emerged as a quality rotational pass-rusher in 2021, and saw his role expand in 2022 and 2023. Reiger missed the 2024 season as a result of a major knee injury that required multiple surgeries, and would then transfer to Wisconsin for his final season. While the Badgers as a whole proved embattled in 2025, Reiger quietly shined, amassing five sacks and six tackles for loss while earning a strong PFSN EDGE Impact score of 84.2 and an excellent true dropback pressure rate of almost 14%, per TruMedia. He'd later earn East-West Shrine Bowl Defensive MVP honors. Reiger's lower sack numbers can partially be explained by a lower pressure conversion rate, which hints at problems with tackling and finishing. But at the core of Reiger's profile are certain athletic and intangible qualities that are sure to help him produce in the NFL. At 6'5", 251 pounds, with near-33" arms, he's tall, explosive, twitched-up, and incredibly nuanced as a pass-rusher. His long-strider explosion alone puts stress on tackles, and fused with his keen angle IQ, he has a vast array of chops, rips, swipes, and push-pulls at his disposal. While his hips can present as somewhat stiff, he has enough ankle flexion to corner while sustaining acceleration, and that same bend helps him acquire leverage in the run game. Reiger needs to improve his finishing ability for conversion efficiency, and he could stand to add mass and improve his pad level maintenance on early downs. He'll likely be restricted to a pass-rush specialist role early on in his career, but Reiger has the athleticism, intelligence, and competitive energy to thrive in that role while he grows in other areas.

name: Andre Fuller
blurb: Andre Fuller is an intriguing Day 3 CB prospect with upside to mold in press-man schemes. A sixth-year senior, Fuller began his career at Arkansas-Pine Bluff in 2020. In 2021, he emerged as an SWAC star, compiling three interceptions and 14 pass breakups. Fuller would then transfer to Toledo, where he played in 28 games across the 2022 and 2023 campaigns as a rotational defender. He was slated for a larger role in 2024, but a core muscle injury stole away his 2024 campaign and cast doubt over his future. With hard work and determination, however, Fuller was able to roar back with a first-team All-MAC 2025 season, racking up 49 tackles, 2.5 tackles for loss, an INT, 11 PBUs, and a PFSN CB Impact score of 84.2. Per TruMedia, he allowed just 0.7 yards per coverage snap, and had a 23.1% forced incompletion percentage. There's clear appeal with Fuller's athletic makeup. Though he has relatively short arms, he's well-built at 6'1", 200 pounds, with elite explosive athleticism, effervescent cylindrical quickness and foot speed, easy hinge fluidity, and propulsive hip sink. Fuller is strong, fast-flowing, and fluid, and he appears in his element when using his mirror-motor to match WRs in press while dishing out targeted jams and jabs. In off-man and zone, Fuller struggles a bit more. He can get tunnel vision on initial route targets and fail to decouple when plays develop, and he can be late to plant-and-drive overtop routes. Additionally, while Fuller's ball production is very good, he can be penalty-prone working with his back to the ball, and he finds himself in that situation relatively often without high-end vertical speed. Tackling is another area of emphasis, though his quickness and ankle mobility aid in angle breakdowns. Best in press-heavy schemes, Fuller functions as quality depth early on, and has quality starter upside.

name: Anez Cooper
blurb: Anez Cooper was one of the unheralded stars of Miami's Championship runner-up offensive line in 2025. He joined the Hurricanes as a three-star recruit in 2022, and started four of ten games played as a true freshman. In 2023 and 2024, he started a combined 26 games at right guard, before reprising that role in a standout 2025 campaign. In 2025, Cooper earned an individual PFSN OL Impact grade of 81.3, while allowing just a 3.3% pressure rate and 10 true dropback pressures, per TruMedia. Cooper's modus operandi in pass protection is simple: Working in a phone booth, he's going to reduce your space and time by winning the contact battle, and then he's going to overwhelm. He can clog space and induce claustrophobia with his size alone, and excels getting hands-on first and using his lead-weight hands to shock and shutter rushes before they can develop. Cooper's stylistic strengths do come at a cost, however. He can lock in on initial rush assignments too often, leaving stunters and late blitzers unattended, and he can be penalty prone on account of failing to replace his hands. Meanwhile, in the run game, Cooper is slow-footed, inexplosive, and displays flexibility limitations. He can drive opponents off the line, seal off gaps, and climb to immediate second-level targets, but is limited as a puller and lead blocker. Best in gap and inside zone schemes, Cooper has a relatively high floor with mid-level starting utility and high-end depth value, but also has a capped ceiling and strict schematic parameters.

name: DeShon Singleton
blurb: DeShon Singleton is a potential sleeper in the 2026 NFL Draft with strong safety appeal. He began his collegiate career at Hutchinson CC, where he played quarterback and safety. After one season among the JUCO ranks, he transferred to Nebraska. He was only a rotational contributor and special teamer in 2022, and missed all but five games due to a knee injury. After coming back from that injury, however, Singleton would go on to start all 25 games across the 2024 and 2025 seasons. In 2025, Singleton was given team captain honors, and accrued 70 tackles, four tackles for loss, two interceptions, and four pass breakups, while compiling a strong PFSN Safety Impact grade of 86. As a two-phase playmaker, Singleton leaves more to be desired. Per TruMedia, he missed 11.1% of his tackle attempts, and logged just a 6.9% forced incompletion percentage in coverage. But dive past the production and efficiency, and you quietly have a solid profile to build on. At 6'2", 205 pounds, with near-33" arms, Singleton has a great size profile, to pair with elite quantified explosiveness and half-field closing range. He's a swift processor with a quick trigger and extremely willing physicality in the run game, and in coverage, he has the versatility to play two-high, rove in the box, or man up against tight ends in the slot. Singleton's fluidity is functional but not elite, and he doesn't quite have the tools to play in press against wide receivers. Additionally, his long speed is capped in space, and he can be more patient and disciplined with his downhill response angles at times. Nevertheless, Singleton has immediate rotational and special teams utility, and could grow into a mid-level starter.

name: Jack Endries
blurb: Jack Endries transferred to Texas after a strong 2024 campaign at California that saw him haul in 56 catches for 623 yards and two scores. He now aims to keep the Longhorns' draft streak at TE alive after Ja'Tavion Sanders was selected in 2023, and Gunnar Helm was selected in 2024. Endries wasn't as productive as expected at Texas in 2025, but the offense's struggles early in the year contributed to this dip in production, and Endries' stock was strong enough that he chose to declare anyway. At around 6'4", 240 pounds, Endries isn't the biggest TE target, but he's quick, explosive, and nuanced as a route runner and stem operator. He has legitimate vertical speed for his size, which he can use to stretch seams, and he's a strong-handed catcher with churning RAC ability. He still has room to improve his play strength and multi-alignment blocking utility, but he has immediate value as a vertical seam and RAC threat in a rotation.

name: Trey Zuhn III
blurb: Trey Zuhn III returned to Texas A&M in 2025 after earning draftable grades from scouts in 2025 as a tackle. In 2025, he earned first-team All-SEC honors while flexing between tackle, guard, and center. Center could ultimately be where Zuhn sticks at the NFL level, but in truth, he has three-position versatility inside, and his experience at tackle serves as an anchor for his utility. Standing at 6'6" and 319 pounds, Zuhn brings imposing size, strength, and impressive foot speed and flexibility. His arm length under 32" is less than ideal, and is a prime driver of Zuhn's likely transition inside, but for a taller lineman, he has the flexibility and leverage acquisition skills to fit in easily playing interior alignments. His quick feet and hands, stout anchor, and competitive edge in both pass protection and the run game insulate his value, and in time, he could be a quality starter at the fulcrum. At the very least, he's an asset as a depth piece.

name: Jack Kelly
blurb: Jack Kelly is a mid-to-late-round LB prospect in the 2026 NFL Draft, with clear run-and-chase value and a track record of production. He began his career at Weber State in 2021, and was a standout for the Wildcats across the 2022 and 2023 seasons. After a 2023 campaign that saw him put up 10.5 sacks and 12 tackles for loss, he transferred to BYU and picked up where he left off. Across two seasons with the Cougars, Kelly totaled 106 tackles, 15 sacks, and 23.5 TFLs. At 6'2", 240 pounds, with 31 1/8" arms, Kelly has above-average size, to pair with swarming sideline-to-sideline range and near-elite on-attack explosion. He plays with tenacious pace and physicality, and weaponizes that unhinged motor both as a gap invader and as an angle-aware blitzer, whose combined explosive range and instincts helped him produce a terrifying 30.2% pressure rate on pass-rush reps in 2025, per TruMedia. While Kelly's attack profile is compelling, his profile does have warts. A 15% missed tackle rate in 2025 hints at a lack of control with angle breakdowns and wrap-up technique, and he can be picked on in coverage with his average fluidity, inconsistent zone feel, and subpar ball skills. Best as an attacking SAM-backer, Kelly has immediate rotational and multi-phase appeal, but needs to shore up his tackling and improve his coverage game before he can be a full-time starter.

name: Josh Cameron
blurb: Josh Cameron had three years of marginal production at Baylor, but he emerged in 2024 with a 52-754-10 receiving line, becoming a favorite target for Bears QB Sawyer Robertson. He followed up that 2024 campaign with a 2025 campaign that saw him achieve career-highs in receptions (69) and yards (872), while earning a respectable 76.8 PFSN WR Impact score. Cameron's late breakout and non-elite efficiency may scare away analytically-minded evaluators, but there's a lot to like on the film. At 6'1", 218 pounds, he's a uniquely-built WR with high-level frame density, compact mass, proportional length, and long-strider burst, but he also has the ability to reduce and use stride variations to separate quickly. He has strong hands at the catch point, and he's built for RAC with his contact balance and burst. Cameron doesn't have field-stretching speed, and his athletic profile isn't elite, but there's a lot of utility in his short and intermediate route tree, chain-moving catch reliability, and active RAC element.

name: Nate Boerkircher
blurb: Nate Boerkircher is a prime candidate to emerge as a "Jackson Hawes" style of late-round TE. He caught just 38 passes in a five-year career at Texas A&M, but distinguished himself in other ways for the Aggies. At around 6'4", 250 pounds, with solid functional length, Boerkircher is built well as a two-phase TE, and he makes his most effusive mark in the blocking phase. He has a functional strength and power profile that can contend right away in the NFL, and he's a tenacious, technically-sound blocker as well. He acquires leverage well and consistently drives his feet through blocks, he understands attack angles, he engages with forceful hands and imposing push, and he's a competent pass protector who can serve as a sixth blocker on passing downs with his steady footwork and anchor. The same functional athleticism that makes Boerkircher so imposing as a moving blocker also underlies some moderate receiving upside; he's quick and explosive for his size, and flashes good hip mobility and stem IQ. That said, his route tree is relatively under-developed, he's not the most flexible catch-point controller, and his hands can be inconsistent, particularly when gathering RAC passes. Boerkircher fits best as a TE2 or TE3 whose prime function is as an immediate run game catalyst.

name: Jalen Huskey
blurb: Jalen Huskey is a gifted playmaker whose turnover production is at the crux of his NFL Draft evaluation. He began his career as a cornerback at Bowling Green, where he earned first-team All-MAC honors as a sophomore after racking up four interceptions and six pass deflections. In 2024, he transferred to Maryland and started 11 games in his first season with the Terrapins, notching three INTs in a breakout Big Ten introduction. In 2025, he played safety full-time for Mike Locksley's squad, registering 72 tackles, 1.5 tackles for loss, four INTs, and three PBUs as well as a glowing PFSN Safety Impact grade of 88.6. At around 6'1", 196 pounds, with sub-31" arms, Huskey has a uniquely tall but short-armed build, and brings decent overarching mobility to the fold. He's not an elite athlete, but he has good baseline burst, fluidity, and quickness, and he's a stellar processor with great reaction speed, route recognition, and spatial reasoning skills from two-high and single-high. Huskey's profile in run support is less appealing; he doesn't play with consistent urgency or dictating physicality when scaling downhill and engaging blocks, and his 12.2% missed tackle rate in 2025 hints at struggles wrapping up and finishing takedowns. Additionally, he visibly maxes out in space when covering more field, and can experience lapses in hip sink and transition efficiency on redirections. With his CB experience, Huskey has value as a versatile depth piece and a backup free safety, but he might max out as a spot-starter in the NFL.

name: Dontay Corleone
blurb: Dontay Corleone, nicknamed "the Godfather", was a standout defender on the CFB stage ever since he set foot on the field at Nippert Stadium. Born and raised in Cincinnati, he had an opportunity to transfer after his three-sack, six-TFL redshirt freshman season, but chose to see out and finish his career with his hometown squad. Later in his career, he never quite recaptured the pass-rushing production that was present early on, but he maintained a presence as a high-quality run defender. Per TruMedia, he allowed just 1.42 yards per run stop in 2025. At 6'0 1/2" and 340 pounds, Corleone is an incredibly unique archetype at the DT position. He's stout and squatty, with stellar mass and proportional length, all of which amounts to superb gap control and power absorption at nose tackle. He's also flashed legitimate power drive and lower-body activation as a pass-rusher, and can at least squeeze the pocket from the fulcrum and force centers to call for reinforcements. Corleone's steady regression as a pass-rusher is concerning, however, and it culminated in a 2025 season where he produced an uninspiring 2% true pressure rate, and failed to log a sack. Certain truths have always been evident with Corleone; he has close to average quickness and flexibility, and those qualities were more limiting in 2025.
