A blood clot issue in 2024 also bears noting, but Corleone has been "all systems go" since. He profiles as an odd-front nose who can function as a tree stump against the run, but is limited with his pass-rush utility.

name: Carson Beck
blurb: Carson Beck was a QB1 candidate entering the 2025 NFL Draft cycle. His 2024 campaign was reasonably productive, and Georgia still went 11-2 under his watch, but a notable regression in accuracy and decision making led Beck to return to school and transfer to Miami for his final season. In his final season, Beck helped lead Miami to a National Championship appearance, but his stock remains tempered. He had surgery to repair the UCL in his right arm after the 2025 season, and his arm simply hasn't been the same since. Arm strength was once a positive of Beck's profile, but it is now a weakness, and it places a greater limitation on his outlook. He has the processing ability and checkdown awareness to be a quality backup, but even as an exclusive pocket passer, he can experience bouts of inaccuracy and arm arrogance that induce volatility. Purely a Day 3 prospect, Beck's experience underlies his value as a QB2 in the NFL.

name: Harrison Wallace III
blurb: Harrison Wallace III is an experienced late-round prospect with intriguing upside as a rotational WR. He first played for the Penn State Nittany Lions, joining the program in 2021 as a four-star recruit. He redshirted in 2021 and produced sparingly in 2022 and 2023, but broke out with a 46-720-4 line in 2024. In 2025, he transferred to Ole Miss, and was a key cog in the team's passing attack in his lone SEC season. Across 15 games, Wallace amassed 61 catches for 934 yards and four touchdowns. He earned a respectable 78.9 PFSN WR Impact score, and per TruMedia, he achieved a reassuring 4.1% drop rate, as well as 2.33 yards per route run. At 6'0", 192 pounds, with 32" arms, Wallace has a uniquely-built frame that grants him impressive reduction ability and hip sink, as well as a relatively wide catch radius. The trade-off with Wallace is that he isn't a high-end vertical athlete. He doesn't quite have the long-track explosion or speed to press and stress cornerbacks consistently. He can also improve at properly using his length to engage in targeted physicality against press and at stems. That said, beyond his top-end athletic limitations, Wallace's profile is fairly well-rounded. He's a well-versed separator with great zone IQ, throttle control, and deceleration working against man coverage. He's a reliable catch-point convertor with adaptable body control, a keen sense of timing, and strong hands. He has some measured RAC ability in open space with his agility and bend, and he's a quality blocker who fulfills his assignments. Wallace may never be a dynamic full-time starter, but he has the length, route running chops, catch-point ability, and blocking value to function as a quality rotational presence, and could take spot-starter reps.

name: Jeremiah Wright
blurb: Jeremiah Wright is a scheme-specific guard prospect in the 2026 NFL Draft. He was a two-way high school player who took reps at both offensive and defensive tackle, and initially, Auburn planned to develop him at DT. He spent 2020 as a reserve DT, and spent 2021 rehabbing from surgery, and shifted back to offensive line ahead of the 2022 season. He spent 2022 and 2023 as a backup and spot-starter at guard, then served as the full-time starter at right guard across 2024 and 2025, earning a Senior Bowl invite for his play in 2025. At 6'5", 331 pounds, with near-34" arms, the most prevalent strength of Wright's profile is his sheer size, contact authority, and power. His wide-set frame presents a challenge for rushers attempting to work across-face, and when Wright is able to latch and set his base, he has elite anchor and core strength. In a phone booth, his hands are incredibly heavy, and he flashes the rotational torque to follow through, finish blocks, and fold gap intruders out of position. For his size, Wright isn't a bad athlete, but his top-end range has a visible cap, and his flexibility is average at best. He has a decent technical floor, particularly in the run game, but he doesn't play with consistent knee bend, and in pass protection, he struggles with over-setting laterally, opening counter lanes for opponents. If Wright can further refine his hand usage and timing, as well as iron out his footwork and angle discipline, he has the domineering build and physicality to serve as high-quality depth in gap, inside zone, and duo-heavy schemes, and a mid-level starting career could be in his future.

name: Lander Barton
blurb: Lander Barton was a prestigious four-star recruit in the 2022 class, who had offers from a host of blue-blood programs including Michigan and Notre Dame. But having played high school football ten minutes from Utah's campus, Barton ultimately chose to play for his hometown school, and he made an instant impact in 2022, amassing eight tackles for loss and 4.5 sacks. Barton never replicated his freshman year production behind the line of scrimmage; a lower-body injury ended his 2023 season and had residual effects in 2024. His 2025 was a decent endcap to a productive career, but questions still remain. At peak form, Barton is a physical and fast-flowing downhill defender with elite competitive urgency and imposing point-of-contact power. He can properly read initial movements, engage, stack blocks, and contend in crowds, and he has actionable blitzing utility as well. Within this mold, however, Barton still has room to add mass to his frame, and a troubling 12% missed tackle rate in 2025 casts doubt over his form and breakdown angle consistency in pursuit. Additionally, he lacks the full-field range to match his short-area explosive element, and while he's a heady coverage player with the length to clog passing lanes, his middling coverage mobility limits his upside on passing downs. Barton projects best as an attacking SAM linebacker and has mid-level starter potential, but his ceiling may be capped.

name: Carver Willis
blurb: Carver Willis is a potential OL sleeper in the 2026 NFL Draft, with predominant experience at tackle but also projected positional flexibility. Willis began his career as a three-star signee at Kansas State in 2020. After playing scarcely from 2020 through 2022, Willis started his first seven games in 2023, and was a full-time starter at right tackle in 2024. In 2025, he transferred to Washington and manned the left tackle position, starting ten games at the blindside. Willis' play earned him invites to the Senior Bowl, where he distinguished himself as one of the most consistent blockers at the event and took reps at center and guard, and to the NFL Combine, where he logged an impressive 5.11 40-yard dash and a 9' broad jump. At around 6'5" and over 300 pounds, with just under 33" arms, Willis is close to average size for an OT, but his size profile holds up much better when projecting inside. He has the requisite experience to be a swing OT right away, but his explosive athleticism, lower-body power and drive, nuanced footwork and hands, and two-phase awareness project well at both guard and center. Additionally, Willis has the flexibility to aid in leverage acquisition and power absorption early in reps, and he's a physical, tenacious finisher. In the run game, Willis functions better with a runway to gear up and align his base on wide zone runs and gap-to-gap pulls, and in pass protection, he can still attain more consistency with his anchor footwork and pad level maintenance. Nevertheless, Willis can function as a high-quality, position-diverse depth piece right away, and he has mid-level starting upside, most likely at guard or center in wide zone schemes.

name: Aaron Anderson
blurb: Aaron Anderson was a four-star recruit in 2022 who signed with Alabama, but didn't see the field in his first season. He would transfer to LSU, and emerged as a top receiving option for the Tigers in 2024, racking up 61 catches for 884 yards and five touchdowns, while earning a PFSN WR Impact grade of 81.5. In 2025, as the entire LSU offense regressed, Anderson quietly remained steady; he maintained a PFSN WR Impact score over 80, and per TruMedia, he achieved an impressive 2.64 yards per route run and 2.79 yards of RAC over expectation. Anderson needs to improve his hands, especially being at a natural size disadvantage, but he's one of the more tantalizing talents in the class for evaluators who favor natural separators and speed threats. As an athlete, Anderson is a hyper-explosive, energized mover with superlative ankle mobility and bend, and he has the speed to stretch seams, compound separation out of breaks, and accrue RAC. That same speed also grants him value as a kick returner, as evidenced by a return TD in 2024. A gasher of off-man and zone, Anderson has legitimate starting upside as a slot WR, but provides immediate utility as a rotational WR, a designed speed and RAC threat, and a special teams presence.

name: Marlin Klein
blurb: Marlin Klein is the latest in a long line of Michigan TE prospects, who could go on to be a more productive pro than college player. Klein played two seasons of high school football in his native-born Germany, before closing out his HS career in Georgia. He signed with the Wolverines as a three-star recruit and toiled in a perpetually deep TE room, but flashed promise in a 13-catch 2024 campaign. In 2025, Klein battled through injuries to play 11 games, in which he totaled 24 catches for 248 yards and his only career touchdown. Klein's profile is defined by dichotomies. He started just 12 games in college, but was a team captain and a quiet staple in a Michigan offense doomed to underperform by instability up top and youth at QB. His production was modest at best, but at 6'6", 248 pounds, with 32 3/8" arms, 4.61 speed, and verifiable explosiveness, he has all of the physical tools required to earn a role as a rotational TE early on in his NFL career. Both as a blocker and a receiver, more refinement is needed. Klein has the requite range, power, and physicality as a blocker, but can tighten his hands and become more consistent with his pad level maintenance. As a receiver, Klein can improve his catch technique consistency and cut down on focus drops, and he can expand on glimpses of quality stem IQ and route running nuance, as well as improve his sturdiness after the catch. But from a bird's eye view, Klein has NFL-caliber size and athleticism, and an immediate two-phase floor as a TE2 with in-line capabilities. How far he progresses depends on his ongoing development.

name: Vincent Anthony Jr.
blurb: Vincent Anthony Jr. is a developmental EDGE prospect set to garner interest in the mid-to-late rounds of the 2026 NFL Draft. Anthony joined the Blue Devils as a four-star recruit in 2022, and proceeded to start 12 of 13 games played in his true freshman season. He was more of a rotational contributor in 2023, before returning to his full-time starting role for the 2024 and 2025 seasons. In 2025, Anthony logged 7.5 sacks and 13 tackles for loss, earning a PFSN EDGE Impact score of 74.5. Anthony's pass-rush element isn't as consistent as his raw production would indicate; per TruMedia, he had a 25% conversion rate on pressures, but only achieved a true dropback pressure rate of 7.2%. That said, the flashes are tantalizing, and Anthony has plenty of tools to mold. At 6'6", 258 pounds, with over 34" arms, he's a long and lean defender with great explosiveness and bend. Though his speed maxes out in pursuit, he has enough burst to run the arc, along with the ankle flexion and torso flexibility to reduce and corner. In spite of his measurements, Anthony still needs to up his play strength and functional power, and while the glimpses of pass-rush execution are compelling, his hand precision and sync can be hot-and-cold. In a similar lens, he has room to play more to his size in run defense. Anthony's profile is one that presents clear strengths and opportunities, but also clear meat left on the bone. In event-front schemes, he has early-career rotational utility, and possesses potential starting upside with further refinement.

name: Deven Eastern
blurb: Deven Eastern is a scheme-specific DT prospect who could command Day 3 interest in the 2026 NFL Draft. He was one of the Golden Gophers' most prized recruits in the 2022 cycle, carrying a four-star designation and a Top 250 national rank. He played in 19 total games across his first seasons as a rotational contributor, before taking on a more consistent starting role in 2024 and 2025. In 2025, Eastern took home a solid PFSN DT Impact score of 76 while stacking up a career-high 2.5 sacks and five tackles for loss. In the process, Eastern earned an invite to the Senior Bowl, where he flashed promise, and to the NFL Combine, where his 9'4" broad jump placed near the 90th percentile at the DT position. At 6'5", 320 pounds, with 34" arms, Eastern has an extremely imposing forklift-power profile, with the near-elite length and compact mass to insulate high-end extension power, stacking strength, and anchor authority. With his sheer body mass and surface area, he can get underneath blocks and sledge opponents upright, thereby minimizing gap displacement and muddying interior looks. Eastern's leveraging isn't consistent enough to support this idealized image on a down-to-down basis; his slight hip stiffness contributes to upright pad level drift, particularly on quick swim attempts. And while he flashes legitimate pass-rush nuance and hand replacement with arm-overs, club-rips, and bull-rushes, his consistency leaves much to be desired here as well. Still, when Eastern is on, the violence of his profile inspires awe. He has the frame, initial explosion, raw strength, and power to potentially hold his own in two-gapping schemes. His stacking power is ultra-reliable for resetting the contact point when his hand placement is sound, and he has the ability to split double-teams and drive his legs to make stops at the line. Eastern is more of a project who will need time to develop deep in a rotation, but he has projected versatility between 1-tech and even-front DT or odd-front DE.

name: Vinny Anthony II
blurb: Vinny Anthony II is a Day 3 WR prospect who projects well as a rotational WR with special teams value. Anthony joined the Wisconsin Badgers in 2022 and stuck with the program across his entire four-year collegiate career. After serving primarily as a special teamer across 2022 and 2023, Anthony broke out with 39 catches for 672 yards and four touchdowns in 2024, earning an 80.2 PFSN WR Impact grade in the process. Anthony's production dipped slightly in 2025, and drops became a bigger issue, but he also flashed quality separation skills and route running nuance in a Wisconsin offense hamstrung by poor scheming and QB play. At around 6'0", 183 pounds, with 31 1/2" arms, Anthony is average-sized, with close to average athleticism. His 4.54 40-yard dash and 34.5" vertical corroborate his lack of high-end speed and burst, but his elite agility numbers paint a different picture for Anthony. While he's not dynamic on the vertical plane, Anthony has excellent short-area quickness, hip fluidity, and bend, which he weaponizes as a route runner with keen angle IQ, stem intelligence, and footwork efficiency. That same blend of agility and spatial intelligence helps Anthony in the RAC phase -- where he averaged 1.62 yards of RAC over expectation in 2025 -- and as a kick return specialist. As a bonus, Anthony gets after it as a blocker, too; he's workmanlike in his approach, physical at the point, and can insert from multiple spots to seal exterior lanes. With his lack of elite athleticism and catch-point prowess, Anthony faces an uphill battle to become a full-time starter, but he has clear depth and rotational value as a movement-Z with blocking and special teams utility.

name: TJ Hall
blurb: TJ Hall is a Day 3 WR prospect who best projects as depth at the NFL level. He played primarily on special teams as a true freshman at Iowa in 2022, and missed half of the 2023 campaign to a season-ending injury. But in 2024, Hall saw an uptick in involvement, starting seven of 12 games played. 2025 was Hall's career-best season; the senior earned third-team All-Big Ten honors while amassing 47 tackles, an interception, and 10 pass breakups. He earned a PFSN CB Impact score of 84.9, and other analytical indicators also rule in Hall's favor; per TruMedia, he established a missed tackle percentage of just 5.7% in support, and had a near-20% forced incompletion percentage at the catch. Hall's limitations were placed under the spotlight at both the Senior Bowl and the NFL Combine, and it's in large part why he let by 368 yards and two scores in coverage in 2025. Hall has average long speed, and is susceptible to stacking from faster WRs. Additionally, at 6'1", Hall isn't the most fluid when swiveling or sinking his hips, and can be prone to gather steps that further delay responses and tangle his plant-and-drive footwork. Hall's best athletic component is his short-range explosion and quickness; for his size, he can match WRs well in close quarters and recalibrate his base, and he's technically sound with good targeted physicality and reaction speed. But even here, shorter proportional arms limit Hall's disruptive reach. Hall's mettle most comes from his football intelligence, schematic versatility between man and zone looks, ball skills, and sturdy run support ability. His athletic limitations and slight inconsistencies with plant-and-drive footwork may prove costly for full-time starter aspirations, but he has depth and rotational utility, as well as situational nickel flexibility.

name: Tanner Koziol
blurb: Tanner Koziol specializes as a premier pass-game weapon in the 2026 NFL Draft TE class. Towering at 6'7", he resembles a power forward on the turf with his height, lean mass, and box-out ability. For his size, he's a plus functional athlete, too. He's fairly fluid as a mover with solid hip flexibility, sink, foot speed, and tempo freedom, all of which proves conducive to independent separation and zone navigation. Koziol can use his smooth mobility and size to throttle up seams and corners, and he proves proficient at using late hands and sharp timing to control positioning in 1-on-1 situations, before capitalizing with body control. In the blocking phase, Koziol is much less consistent; he stands to add more mass, power, and play strength, as his sustain can suffer post-contact. That said, at the very least, he has usage versatility. He can block off motions and insert into interior gaps, or get out in space, and for a taller TE, he has the bend and flexibility to properly acquire leverage. In the immediate timeline, Koziol is a receiving TE first, with smooth mobility and fairly sound hands, but there is blocking upside present if he can improve his presence at contact.

name: Wydett Williams Jr.
blurb: Wydett Williams is a potential Day 3 gem in the 2026 NFL Draft, with a strong production profile. He began his career at Delta State, where he played two seasons before transferring to UL Monroe in 2024. In one year with the Warhawks, Williams amassed 99 tackles, 4.5 tackles for loss, a sack, three interceptions, and four pass breakups. Along the way, he earned the interest of Ole Miss, and in one year with the Rebels, he made similar headway. In 2025, Williams accrued 73 tackles, three INTs, and 10 PBUs, and took home the third-highest PFSN Safety Impact score among all FBS safeties, with an elite figure of 93.4, as well as a forced incompletion percentage of 23.4%. At 6'1 3/8" and 200 pounds, with near-32" arms, Williams has good size, but also visible athletic confinements. While he has an above-average athletic floor, Williams isn't overly explosive, nor does he have great long speed. He's quick, twitched-up, and swift to coil and recover hip alignments, and he has good baseline fluidity, but the chief elevator of Williams' game isn't his athleticism, but his elite processing profile. Williams' reaction speed and urgency aid in maximizing his play pace in both phases. In coverage, he's elite at managing space between route concepts in two-high, and has the reactive athleticism and in-the-moment adaptability to maintain hip leverage and proper positioning. Meanwhile, in support, while he can be inconsistent converting as a tackler, he's fast-to-trigger, angle-sound, and tenacious engaging blocks. Williams' measured athletic limitations likely confine him to a pure split-field safety role, without nickel versatility. But in his role, Williams has immediate rotational appeal, and could become a quality starter in a scheme that adheres to his best qualities.

name: Adam Randall
blurb: Adam Randall joined the Clemson Tigers as a highly anticipated four-star wide receiver recruit back in 2022. But after hauling in just 48 catches for 533 yards and two touchdowns over his first three seasons, he made the switch to running back in 2025, and it ended up being a career-defining move. Randall ran for 814 yards and 10 TDs on 168 carries, and caught 36 passes for 254 yards and three scores -- eclipsing 1,000 yards from scrimmage for the first time in his career -- and earned a stellar 83.1 PFSN RB Impact grade. As one might expect from a young player in just his first year at a position full-time, Randall's vision and creative instincts are very raw; when he has less space to use and less time to react, he can struggle. However, in space, Randall has the explosiveness, speed, and churning leg drive to chew up space and finish with physicality. He flashes above-average press and stem IQ when approaching blocks 1-on-1, and with his WR background, he's a naturally versatile pass-catching threat, who also proves competent as a blocker. In the immediate timeline, Randall serves as a versatile rotational piece, but he has exciting upside if the RB position can slow down for him with more experience.

name: Billy Schrauth
blurb: Billy Schrauth is a scheme-specific Day 3 guard prospect, who could go on to exceed his draft capital in the right situation. Schrauth originally joined the Notre Dame Fighting Irish as a four-star recruit in 2022. He redshirted his first season, then spent most of 2023 as a rotational contributor, starting the season's final three games at right guard. He started 12 games in 2024, and started seven more at left guard in 2025 while serving as a team captain, before declaring for the 2026 NFL Draft. Schrauth's medicals demand extra homework; he missed time in 2024 due to a high ankle sprain, and again missed a stretch of games in 2025 with an MCL sprain. But for the teams who clear Schrauth, he's an intriguing potential value on Day 3 in gap-heavy schemes. At 6'5", 310 pounds, with near-33" arms, Schrauth has a well-put-together frame with excellent density and a strong anchor. In the run game, he has enough initial explosiveness to reach adjacent landmarks on reach, down, and base blocks, and has the violent rotational torque to fully extend and seal defenders out of lanes. Meanwhile, as a pass protector, Schrauth's anchor persists as a reliable strength. He's synergetic and sturdy against power, processes blitzes and stunts at a high level, and has the independent hand usage and carriage recalibration to gather and stymy rushers as they work across-face. The concerns with Schrauth derive from his ultimate ceiling. While he's explosive enough, he can be heavy-footed in recovery, and lacks inspiring second-level range. His hips are relatively stiff, which can further impede recovery and cause upright pad level drift, and he at times struggles to sustain lower-body power exertions. Best utilized in a scheme where he can operate across adjacent gaps and in a phone booth, Schrauth has mid-level starting upside at his peak, but functions well as quality depth in the immediate timeline.

name: Lewis Bond
blurb: Lewis Bond developed from an unheralded three-star recruit in 2021 to an ultra-dependable primary option for the Boston College Eagles in 2025. In his final season, he accmulated 88 catches, 993 yards, and a touchdown, and he also put together a strong analytical profile. His PFSN WR Impact grade was a rock-solid 80.5, and per TruMedia, Bond also registered 2.29 yards per route run, a catch rate over expectation of 13.88%, and a miniscule 2.7% drop rate. Despite being under 5'11" and 190 pounds, with sub-30" arms, Bond plays up to and beyond his size with his catch-point composure and hand strength, and he's an incredibly nuanced route runner and separator with high-level hip fluidity and flexibility underpinning his separation framework. At his size, Bond lacks ideal functional athleticism, as his speed and explosiveness measure closer to average than elite. That said, he's an exceedingly sound football player both before and at the catch, and he has the thickly-built midsection and lower body to supplement play strength in the RAC phase as well. A likely mid-to-late-round target, Bond has immediate rotational appeal, and could be a future starting slot WR.

name: Jadon Canady
blurb: Jadon Canady is a versatile Day 3 prospect with enticing positional flexibility and multi-phase utility, who's long been ahead-of-schedule with his playmaking gifts. After snatching nine interceptions as a high school senior, he signed with Tulane and immediately produced a true freshman, with two INTs and 2.5 tackles for loss in 12 games played. He kept his starting boundary CB job in 2022, but tore his ACL and meniscus eight games into the season. Canady transferred to Ole Miss in 2023 and spent most of the 2023 season rehabbing from his injury, but returned to the fold as a full-time starter, amassing 12 pass breakups while shifting between CB and safety roles. In 2025, he transferred to Oregon and maintained his value as a versatile hybrid. At around 5'10", 182 pounds, with just over 30" arms, Canady's size will be scrutinized, especially in nickel roles that have high physical demands. Nevertheless, Canady projects well as an immediate depth piece and potential high-floor flex DB with near-universal positional application on the back end. Canady can play split-field safety, nickel, or boundary CB. He has the elite short-area quickness and recalibration athleticism to hold his own in man coverage, as well as the smooth pedal, technical variability, keen processing, and fluid transitions to manage space and respond efficiently in off-man and zone. In run support, he sometimes overshoots breakdown angles due to aggression and can stand to play with more control, but no one will question his urgency, his trigger, or his willingness to play above his weight class in contact situations. Adding weight will serve Canady well at the NFL level, but with a bit more mass, he has immediate rotational value and quality starter potential, with the versatility to be a melding piece in the secondary.

name: James Brockermeyer
blurb: James Brockermeyer is an experienced Day 3 center prospect who projects as quality depth in the immediate timeline, but could take on starting reps in relief when required to. Brockermeyer was originally a four-star recruit from Texas, who played for the Alabama Crimson Tide from 2021 to 2023. He played sparingly in 2021 and 2022, and eventually transferred to TCU in 2024, where he was a full-time starter at center. He reprised that role at Miami in 2025, and earned first-team All-American honors while serving as the anchor of one of the best offensive lines in the nation. For his play in 2025, Brockermeyer earned a strong PFSN OL Impact grade of 84.7, and allowed just nine pressures on true dropbacks, per TruMedia. At around 6'3", 297 pounds, with 32" arms, Brockermeyer fits the mold of a stout, squatty, well-leveraged center. While his functional athleticism is decent and better than his testing indicates, Brockermeyer most separates himself with his experience, awareness, and strong technical floor. Brockermeyer has great synergetic feel and timing in pass protection, with the requisite strength to latch and anchor after initial rushes. In the run game, he's not a rangy zone blocker, but he's dutiful, well-leveraged, and assignment-sound in tight spaces, with the urgency and physicality to attack his assignments with zeal. While Brockermeyer is physical, he's not built to dish out high-end hand power on extensions, and he can exhibit slight lapses in square positioning and hip flexibility on recovery in pass protection. Best confined to gap and inside zone schemes, Brockermeyer can insulate a line's depth right away, and a respectable spot-starter career is likely in his future.

name: Rayshaun Benny
blurb: Rayshaun Benny comes from a Michigan program known for producing quality defensive line talent, and will likely hear his name called on Day 3 of the 2026 NFL Draft. Benny joined the Wolverines as a four-star recruit in 2021, and waited his turn to produce while playing behind Mazi Smith, Chris Hinton, and later Mason Graham and Kenneth Grant. During Michigan's National Championship campaign in 2023, Benny took on a primary rotational role alongside Graham and Grant, and would earn a career-high 5.5 tackles for loss. He never eclipsed that number or 1.5 sacks over the next two seasons, but compiled several years of quality film at a Big Ten program. At 6'3", 298 pounds, with near-34" arms, Benny has a tantalizing blend of natural leverage, compact mass, and proportional length, along with the initial explosive capacity to siphon additional acceleration and power output at contact. His stacking strength and full extension as a one and two-gapper pops on film, and at the Senior Bowl, Benny flashed as a pass-rusher with his burst, motor, and measured quickness. He's much more explosive than agile, and despite his natural leverage, his high-hipped frame causes frequent upright pad level drift, and contributes to moderate stiffness on lateral transitions. Those factors combine to limit Benny's pass-rushing utility past initial power and contain roles, and that same upright drift can make him easy to sledge off the line by combo blocks. Benny's ceiling is somewhat unclear, but he should be able to function as a quality rotational player in even and hybrid-front schemes, with a respectable run defense profile.

name: Will Kacmarek
blurb: Will Kacmarek spent his first three seasons at Ohio, before transferring to play for the regional blue-blood: The Ohio State Buckeyes. At Ohio State, he served as a quality TE2 for two seasons, peaking in 2025 with 15 catches for 168 yards and a respectable PFSN TE Impact score of 76.7. Kacmarek never emerged as a starting talent at Ohio State, and lost reps to Max Klare in the passing game. That said, Kacmarek maintained his value as a high-quality blocking presence. At 6'5", 259 pounds, with plus length and play strength, Kacmarek sizes up well in contact situations, and while his leverage, pad level maintenance, and leg drive post-contact can be more consistent, he engages with force, is capable of operating as a sixth pass-protector, and can be difficult for defenders to unlatch. As a receiver, Kacmarek's upside is visibly capped. Athletically, he's not overly explosive, fluid, or balanced, with visible hip stiffness that constricts his route tree, and he's not very flexible in contortion situations. That said, Kacmarek's arm reach and strong hands provide measured value in red-zone and conversion scenarios. Best as a late-round pick or PFA, Kacmarek projects as a rotational TE and primary blocking TE in heavy personnel sets, whose toughness and role versatility set a stable floor.

name: Cade Klubnik
blurb: Cade Klubnik is a former five-star recruit and historic high school producer, who grew to become one of the more prolific passers in college football. His senior season was a notable regression, however, that saw him put up inconsistent film as a processor and field general down-over-down. At 6'2", 210 pounds, he's not quite the big-armed prototypical passer you'd expect in contention for early-round capital, but he's nonetheless a very compelling talent. At his best, he's an electric point guard-style passer in the mold of Bo Nix, with dynamic athleticism, universal off-platform throwing ability, and endless arm angle freedom. However, his abundance of physical talent is marred by a lack of consistent post-snap discernment, field vision, and anticipation, and his situational accuracy took a step back in 2025. Klubnik still holds mid-round appeal as an ideal backup in West Coast-style schemes, but he's a developmental QB in the mental category, who might not get the development he needs at the next level. Nevertheless, if he can expand on the flashes of leverage IQ and spatial awareness, he has starting upside.

name: Collin Wright
blurb: Collin Wright is a late-round or PFA CB prospect in the 2026 NFL Draft. He played four years at Stanford, signing as a four-star recruit ranked at both cornerback and safety in the 2022 cycle. After redshirting his true freshman season, Wright started all 12 games in 2023, and reprised that full-time starting role in 2024 and 2025, while shifting between CB and nickel. In total, Wright accrued 10 tackles for loss, a sack, five interceptions, and 14 pass breakups across his collegiate career. Later, he would compete at the Senior Bowl, and attend the NFL Combine, where he logged an impressive 39" vertical. Wright doesn't always play up to that athleticism on film, and that's one of his bigger criticisms. As his pro day 40-yard dash of 4.51 corroborates, he lacks high-end vertical or recovery speed, and at around 6'0", 188 pounds, with 30 3/4", his close-to-average size doesn't counteract that. To his credit, Wright does have great coverage mobility; he's fairly twitched-up, reactive, and fluid, with excellent processing ability, reactive coil, route recognition, and technical variability. Particularly in situations where targeted physicality and playmaking is required, however, Wright can struggle. He can be off-set and displaced in press coverage, and his eye discipline and precision at the catch point are inconsistent. Those issues are exacerbated when he doesn't have the recovery speed to consistently regain positioning. Nevertheless, Wright's experience, versatility, and coverage mobility insulate his stock, and he also has quality run support ability, with good play recognition, block take-on, and tackling ability. Broadly, Wright is a scheme-diverse depth CB with a deference to off-man and zone. His playmaking inconsistencies and non-elite athleticism lower his ceiling, but he has immediate rotational utility and can serve as a replacement-level starter with boundary-nickel flex.

name: Diego Pounds
blurb: Diego Pounds is a Day 3 offensive line prospect with an abundance of experience and scheme-specific upside. He transitioned from defensive line to offensive line as a junior in high school and later joined the North Carolina Tar Heels as a three-star recruit. He played in five games as a true freshman and started eight games as a sophomore, then transferred to Ole Miss ahead of the 2024 campaign. With the Rebels, Pounds anchored the left tackle spot, starting nine games in 2024, and then serving as a full-time starter across Ole Miss' CFB semifinal run. At 6'6", 325 pounds, with near-34" arms, Pounds size, strength, and power are his most exciting traits. Those natural obstructing qualities, combined with his experience, make him a venerable foe, particularly in pass protection. He's smooth and balanced in his pass set, he has the matching footwork and angle IQ to stay square and mediate rushing paths, he's patient and timely with his hands, and he has great synergetifc feel and anchor strength. While he's explosive for his size, he's relatively slow-footed and inflexible in the run game, which limits his range and arsenal of blocks strictly to base blocks and play-side down blocks, and impedes lower-body drive. Additionally, while Pounds has good technical utility, he can still improve his leverage acquisition and pad level maintenance later in reps, as his knee bend sometimes fades with weight transfers. Pounds will likely be confined to power, gap, and inside zone schemes, but he has immediate depth value and mid-level starting utility at tackle, and could translate well at guard with his domineering size and strength.

name: Tim Keenan III
blurb: Tim Keenan III was a three-year starter for the Alabama Crimson Tide, after first joining the program as a four-star recruit in 2021. In the offseason of 2023, he trimmed down weight and became a mainstay in a stacked defensive line rotation, and he carried that momentum forward with a standout 2024 campaign, wherein he earned 2.5 sacks, 7.5 tackles for loss, and a PFSN DT Impact grade of 77.3. Keenan's 2025 was a slight regression compared to 2024, but still, his run defense utility and line-resetting linear power and strength remained at the forefront. The Senior Bowl spotlighted both these prevailing strengths, as well as his limited pass-rush profile. Keenan is a high-motor defender, and when he gets pressure, he's good at converting. But at 6'1", 333 pounds, Keenan is stiff and short-armed, and doesn't have a consistent counter arsenal or a well-developed rush plan beyond initial power. At the very least, he has the linear burst to supplement power and play strength against solo blocks and combos, and his natural leverage profile plays well against double-teams, even if he can be sledged upright at times. There's some additional pass-rush upside to unearth with Keenan's measured burst and foot quickness at his size, but his ceiling could ultimately be capped by his lacking length and midsection stiffness. At his best, he can be a mid-level starting nose tackle, but he may more often serve as a rotational piece in odd and hybrid-front schemes.

name: Jakobe Thomas
blurb: Jakobe Thomas is a quietly well-rounded Day 3 safety prospect with a clear ceiling but also universal utility that could help him see the field as a rotational defender at least, and a mid-level starter at best. Thomas began his career as a three-star signee for Middle Tennessee State, and after redshirting his first season, he emerged with a four-interception 2022 campaign. After three years at MTSU, Thomas had a one-year interlude with the Tennesse Volunteers, before closing out his career with the Miami Hurricanes in 2025. Whatever production he sought but lacked in the SEC, Thomas reclaimed in the ACC. In 2025, he accumulated 76 tackles, four tackles for loss, 3.5 sacks, five interceptions, six pass breakups, and two forced fumbles, while earning a glowing PFSN Safety Impact score of 88.4. Thomas' turnover production stands out most, and it's emblematic of his ability to manage depth and space from two-high or single-high, read-and-react, and break in front of passes. In coverage, Thomas has good recalibration quickness and fluidity, and while his top-end speed is capped, he has enough half-field range to be disruptive when his breaks are well-timed. In run support, Thomas has similar playmaking urgency, and triggers downhill quickly, with the length and mass to hold up taking on blocks. A missed tackle percentage over 15% reflects a need to exercise better timing, breakdown discipline, and technique at contact, but Thomas nonetheless has value as a fast-flowing and physical downhill presence. Thomas can bite prematurely against Cover 2 beaters, his plant-and-drive footwork and transition balance can be more consistent at times, and he's not as reliable tracking the ball when making up ground vertically. Those inconsistencies are exacerbated by a lack of high-end long speed or recovery burst. Having said all this, Thomas has a respectable floor across the board, and can function well as a rotational and depth safety with spot-starting utility in two-deep schemes.

name: Bryce Boettcher
blurb: Bryce Boettcher is a mid-to-late-round LB prospect with immediate depth and special teams utility, as well as potential starter upside. A two-sport star, Boettcher was drafted in the 13th round of the 2024 MLB Draft, but the two-year Oregon Ducks starter and Senior Bowl standout ultimately chose football as his professional sport, and he'll be aiming for higher capital in the 2026 NFL Draft. After walking on and primarily as a special teams and rotational contributor in 2022 and 2023, Boettcher emerged as a full-time starter for the Ducks in 2024, and retained that role through 2025. Across that stretch, Boettcher totaled 230 tackles, 13.5 tackles for loss, three sacks, two interceptions, 10 pass breakups, and three forced fumbles, while earning second-team All-Big Ten honors in both years. At around 6'1", 233 pounds, with sub-31" arms, Boettcher has below-average size, and lacks quantifiably elite athleticism. Still, he has solid compact mass for his frame, as well as the requisite explosiveness, quickness, and range to manage to correct his positioning and chase at the second level. In spite of his size, he's incredibly physical and is willing to proactively engage downhill into contact, he's a stellar processor with good pre-snap and post-snap recognition, and he's a competent zone coverage defender with the ball tracking one would expect from a player with a baseball background. A potential mid-level starter and valuable depth piece at a relatively low opportunity cost, Boettcher stands as one of the better value acquisitions in the draft.

name: Jeff Caldwell
blurb: Jeff Caldwell is a potential sleeper in the 2026 NFL Draft with a compelling size-speed profile to mold. A late bloomer in high school, Caldwell experienced a senior-year growth spurt that set him behind in the recruiting circuit, and was only given one Division I offer from Lindenwood. He played three years with the Lions, peaking in 2024 with 52 catches for 1,011 yards and 10 touchdowns. Averaging over 18 yards per catch across his three years in the FCS, Caldwell leveraged his production into a year at Cincinnati. With the Bearcats, he amassed 32 catches for 478 yards and six scores. His 77.7 PFSN WR Impact score was around middle-of-the-pack, but his 2.4 yards per route run hinted at additional efficiency to extract. At 6'5", 216 pounds, with near-33" arms, Caldwell's most enthralling quality is his size-adjusted athleticism. At the NFL Combine, he quantified this with a blistering 4.31 40-yard dash, a 1.48 10-yard split, a 42" vertical, and an 11'2" broad jump. Caldwell's size, speed, and burst set him apart, but the rest of his profile still needs work. He has excellent contortion ability at the catch point, but can be prone to focus drops and inconsistency through contact, and he has room to further excel at controlling the catch point with timing and coordination. His route tree is primarily vertical, and while he has good building blocks, his plant-and-drive footwork and sink can be very inconsistent. Additionally, Caldwell provides relatively little as a RAC threat. The saving grace for Caldwell is this; he's a competent run blocker with the tools and temperament to translate. As it stands, he has actionable rotational value as a designated deep threat and possesses run-game implementation, but he needs to refine his three-level profile in order to become more.

name: Fernando Carmona Jr.
blurb: Fernando Carmona Jr. transferred to Arkansas from San Jose State in 2024, after starting two full seasons at left tackle and earning honorable mention All-Mountain West recognition in 2023. Upon arriving at Arkansas, he began his tenure with the Razorbacks at LT, but a streaky acclimation to SEC play led him to kick inside to left guard for his final season. At guard, Carmona looked more comfortable -- registering an elite PFSN OL Impact grade of 91 -- and that's likely where he'll stick at the NFL level. Carmona's linear athleticism pops, and he has hyper-elite raw power capacity when he's able to work on the attack as a puller and climber. He's not shy about finishing opponents in the dirt, either -- though he can employ better discipline at times (10 penalties in 2025). He's not overly flexible; he especially lacks in knee bend, and he needs to improve his leverage acquisition and pad level in pass protection. Still, Carmona brings experience, explosive straight-line athleticism, menacing power capacity, and relentless physicality. With improvements in leverage play and hand replacement, he can be an effective starter inside, with a particular affinity for gap schemes.

name: Tyreak Sapp
blurb: Tyreak Sapp might not be the highest-ranked Florida defender on PFSN's board, but he was the heart and soul of the Gators defense in 2024, with 7 sacks, 13 TFLs, and 2 forced fumbles to his name. His production stalled in 2025, but he still has a compelling outlook for evaluators, with projected alignment versatility. At 6'3", 275 pounds, he's a compact, high-mass, well-leveraged edge defender with fleet-footed acceleration, smooth lateral agility, inspiring ankle flexion and bend capacity, imposing strength at contact, and a red-hot motor on all three downs. With his sawed-off build, Sapp does bring less-than-ideal length to the table, which limits his maximum raw power capacity at times. To that end, his margin for error is slimmer against longer blockers, and he's still refining his hand usage and precision. Nevertheless, the build, the motor, and the upward trajectory sells his stock.

name: John Michael Gyllenborg
blurb: John Michael Gyllenborg joined the Wyoming Cowboys as a three-star recruit in 2021 and redshirted his first season with the team. After taking time to build up his frame in 2022, Gyllenborg's production peaked across the 2023 and 2024 seasons, where he combined for 53 catches, 785 yards, and six touchdowns across that two-year stretch. His production dipped in 2025, but Gyllenborg still has the physical gifts to earn the allure of NFL teams. At around 6'5", 251 pounds, Gyllenborg is tall and lean, with elite explosive athleticism. He can get upfield in a blink as a seam threat or working off motions, and he has the long speed to stretch creases and get behind the second level. Additionally, with his tall frame, he's shown he can properly box out defenders, play positioning, and secure tough catches. Outside of his value as a seam and vertical threat, Gyllenborg's game has its question marks. While he's tall and well-built, his arms are short; this reduces his catch radius and doesn't play well with his inconsistent leverage and pad level as a blocker. On early downs, Gyllenborg's utility remains limited, but if he can stay healthy, he has the explosive athleticism, ankle mobility, receiving chops, and play pace worth banking on in the later rounds.

name: Jamarion Miller
blurb: Jamarion Miller was given a chance to emerge as a lead back in 2025 and failed to do so definitively. In fact, 2025 featured his worst efficiency yet as a runner, and cast doubt on his ultimate NFL upside. Nonetheless, Miller has clear value as a utility player, and has flashed legitimate NFL traits. Despite tipping the scales at 220 pounds, he's an uber-explosive runner with slashing lateral burst and eye-catching angle freedom and flexibility on one-cut plays. That same angle freedom shows up as a route runner, where he's displayed the ability to off-set defenders and create separation on angle routes and other quick concepts. To top it off, Miller is a near-elite blocker with unmatched competitive energy. His inconsistent vision and spatial awareness limit his upside as a volume back and confine him to a rotational role, but his multi-phase utility is valuable enough that he deserves a spot in a rotation.

name: Sawyer Robertson
blurb: Sawyer Robertson was one of the more productive quarterbacks on the college football stage over the past two seasons. Starting four games in 2023, Robertson's game was much less consistent over that early-career stretch, but he returned as a passer reimagined in 2024, throwing for over 3,000 yards and almost 30 scores while leading his team to a 7-4 record. In 2025, Robertson achieved career-highs in yardage (3,681) and passing TDs (31), and earned a spot on the Panini Senior Bowl roster. As a likely Day 3 selection, Robertson has plenty of appealing qualities. Though his arm is above-average at best, he's a prototypical passer with very good creation capacity and underlying touchness, and with an extra year of starting experience, his pre-snap and post-snap vision, as well as his mechanical control, markedly improved in 2025. He can still experience shoulder misalignment and situational imprecision at times, and will attempt to force throws his arm doesn't quite have the juice for, but he has the baseline physical talent, mental acuity, and toughness of an ideal backup QB and safety blanket.

name: Devan Boykin
blurb: Devan Boykin is an under-the-radar defensive back prospect in the 2026 NFL Draft, with both nickel and safety functionality. Boykin began his career as a three-star cornerback signee at NC State in 2020, and played in 11 games as a true freshman. Over the next three seasons, he'd start 18 of 33 games played while serving as a versatile rotational piece for the Wolfpack, achieving a career-high three interceptions in 2023. A torn ACL suffered at the end of the 2023 season ultimately caused Boykin to miss the entire 2024 campaign, and he would later transfer to Indiana to join Curt Cignetti's Championship squad. In his lone year with the Hoosiers, Boykin produced 51 tackles, six tackles for loss, a sack, two INTs, five pass breakups, two fumble recoveries, and a forced fumble. He logged a strong PFSN Safety Impact score of 80.8, and per TruMedia, he logged an excellent missed tackle percentage of just 6.1%. Boykin's positional versatility is a hallmark of his profile. He can play nickel or safety in split-field looks, and he's borderline elite in run support, with a quick-firing trigger, sharp angle IQ, excellent block take-on technique, and finishing ability. Those same intangibles help him in coverage, where he combines fluid, fleet-footed, and agile athleticism with measured spatial management and keen route vision. Being below-average size with shorter arms, Boykin can be overmatched at stems, and he doesn't have elite recovery burst or speed. He'll turn 25 years old late in his rookie season, and he's undersized without elite testing athleticism or playmaking production. Nevertheless, for win-now teams or growing teams in need of versatility and cohesion-lending skills on the back end, Boykin makes a lot of sense as a late-round target. He projects as an immediate high-quality depth piece with nickel and safety flex, and he could eventually take reps as a mid-level starter in zone-heavy schemes.

name: Red Murdock
blurb: Khalil "Red" Murdock's nickname fits: He sees red on the football field. It's one reason he became one of the most productive collegiate linebackers in recent memory. Murdock's ability to impact the game was clear as early as high school, where he helped lead Hopewell High to a state championship in Virginia, while completing a dual enrollment associate's degree. But injuries contributed to Murdock's interest fizzling out, and Buffalo was his only FBS offer. He scarcely played in 2022, but started six games in 2023, and was a full-time starter from that point on. The numbers speak for themselves: Across three seasons, Murdock accumulated 358 total tackles, 39.5 tackles for loss, nine sacks, and a whopping 17 forced fumbles, which constitutes a FBS record. Murdock's production can't be denied, but his NFL projection is admittedly unclear. He's around average size at 6'1", 236 pounds, with just 31 1/8" arms, and he's a middling athlete with clear speed and burst limitations. In the MAC, he was able to counteract his lacking athleticism with swift processing, size-defying power output at contact, and unhinged on-attack physicality and urgency, and he has the angle IQ to provide value as a blitzing threat. Those counteractive measures will have to hold at the NFL level, if he is to carry a regular defensive role. At the very least, Murdock is an excellent locker room addition and has immediate special teams value.

name: Caden Barnett
blurb: Caden Barnett is a physical, well-built interior offensive line prospect with upside as a high-quality depth player and scheme-specific starter candidate. He originally signed with the Wyoming Cowboys as a three-star recruit in 2021 and redshirted his first season. In 2022, he rose up the depth chart as a regular rotational player, and started 10 games in 2023. In 2024, he was a full-time starter at offensive tackle, before shifting inside to guard for the 2025 campaign. At around 6'4", 316 pounds, with 33 3/8" arms, Barnett has a compelling build on the interior. He's stout, well-leveraged, and long-limbed, with an incredibly strong anchor. His game revolves around making contact quickly and using his combined explosive athleticism, strength, power profile, and strength to reset the point while using his anchor to minimize displacement. In the run game, he can reach landmarks quickly, acquire leverage, and fully extend to seal opponents out of gaps or unleash rotational torque. And in pass protection, his stout anchor and low center of gravity makes him difficult to work through. Having said all this, Barnett's game has its warts. In the run game, his lack of short-area agility and leg action shows up at times when he fails to drive through blocks, and in pass protection, while his anchor is generally sound, his hands can be slow and inefficient on replacement, and he can be late to recognize stunts and reposition his base accordingly. Barnett's experience at tackle and guard immediately gives him strong depth utility in gap and duo-heavy schemes, but he'll need to further refine his hand usage and adaptability to realize his mid-level starter potential.

name: Romello Brinson
blurb: Romello Brinson is a late-round or PFA WR prospect with an intriguing blend of size and late-career production. He began his career with the Miami Hurricanes as a four-star signee in 2021, but after two seasons of marginal production, he chose to transfer to SMU. With the Mustangs, he achieved a greater level of production, peaking in 2025 with 43 catches for 638 yards and three touchdowns. While his 74.7 PFSN WR Impact score was average, he logged a catch rate over expectation of 3.33%, and averaged 2.67 yards of RAC over expectation per catch. At 6'1", 177 pounds, Brinson is tall and lean, with good proportional length and a competent but non-elite athletic skill set. He's fast and explosive enough as a long-strider to stretch intermediate seams and threaten vertical, and while he's not overly twitched-up or fluid, he's shown he can stem defenders in space, manipulate with head fakes and angle oscillations, and flow into open zones. In the RAC phase, Brinson weaponizes his explosiveness with quick reaction speed and smooth spatial IQ, and at the catch, while drops can be an issue, he's shown he can make quick-reaction plays with body control, contortion, and extended reach. Brinson's lighter mass profile, non-elite deep speed, catch technique inconsistency, and slight hip flexibility limitations put a cap on his ceiling, but he has the well-rounded skill set and RAC utility to be a competent depth WR with rotational appeal, and his quality blocking ability adds to his value in that lens.

name: Caden Curry
blurb: Caden Curry is a late-round or PFA prospect in the 2026 NFL Draft, who has limited traits but the motor and penchant for production to hold a place on the depth chart. He spent his entire career with the Ohio State Buckeyes, first signing as a four-star recruit in 2022. Right away, he earned a spot in the rotation, totaling 4.5 sacks and 8.5 tackles for loss from 2022 to 2024, while taking reps from multiple alignments and serving on special teams. In 2025, Curry broke out as the team's most disruptive threat off the edge, accumulating 11 sacks and 16.5 TFLs in 14 games. Curry's experience and late-career production are positive indicators, but his NFL projection is a bit more complicated. At 6'3", 257 pounds, Curry has good natural leverage and compact mass, qualities that helped him moonlight as a situational power rusher from 3-tech and 4i in addition to taking reps outside. But at that size, Curry also has extremely short arms. He's reasonably explosive, but doesn't have an elite first step, he's not a pass-rush technician, and he's also relatively stiff, both with his attempted cornering and hip flexibility working past blocks. The appeal with Curry comes from this: His motor is relentless, he's strong, well-leveraged, and gap-sound in run defense, and his unhinged chase mentality ensures that he'll always be around the ball when turned loose. Curry will likely never be an NFL starter, but he has functionality as an alignment-versatile rotational presence with high-quality run defense, a reliable leverage profile, and motorized playmaking in pursuit.

name: DJ Rogers
blurb: DJ Rogers profiles as one of the more well-rounded two-phase TE prospects in the 2026 NFL Draft. He joined the Horned Frogs in 2021 and redshirted that year, and only caught seven passes through his first three seasons. The 2024 campaign saw Rogers become more involved as a primary TE, but he saved his best for last in 2025, catching 34 passes for 319 yards and two touchdowns, while putting up a PFSN TE Impact score of 78.6. Rogers grades out as solid but unspectacular in most areas. He doesn't have elite explosion, speed, or quickness, but at least passes the requisite threshold in all athletic categories. As a route runner, he has a solid route tree and very good functional stem IQ, with the range to threaten vertically up seams and accrue RAC with creases of space. And as a blocker, while he can still improve his play strength a bit, he has a good power profile, excels at squaring up his assignments and acquiring leverage, and is competent as an extra pass protector when called upon. With his non-elite athleticism, size, and at-times inconsistent hands, Rogers might never be a starting TE, but he can be a very good TE2 with multi-phase utility.

name: Cameron Ball
blurb: Cameron Ball is a late-round DT prospect with a compelling physical profile, but also untapped potential relative to his experience level. He joined the Arkansas Razorbacks as a three-star recruit in 2021, and stayed in Fayetteville all five years. After redshirting in 2021, he became a regular rotational contributor in 2022 and 2023, before serving as a full-time starter across the 2024 and 2025 seasons. Across a four-year on-field career, Ball accumulated 138 tackles, 12.5 tackles for loss, and three sacks, and earned a respectable PFSN DT Impact score of 80.6 in 2025. At 6'4", with a playing weight of close to 320 pounds and 33" arms, Ball has ideal size, strength, and power capacity on the interior, and he's an excellent athlete as well, as his NFL Combine showing reaffirmed. His 32" vertical, in particular, is above the 88th percentile at his position. Ball is also a team leader and active in his community; he was a Wuerffel Trophy watch list inclusion in the lead-up to the 2025 season. Those combined factors alone should help Ball exist in a rotational role early on, but his development beyond that remains polarizing. At his best, Ball flashed near-elite stacking strength and functional two-gapping potential in run defense at Arkansas, and his explosive athleticism lends well to initial power output. That said, his pad level and leverage acquisition can be very inconsistent, and frequent upright drift can negate his base and make him easier to move off the line. He gives up hip-to-gap leverage too easily at times as well, and as a pass-rusher, he remains extremely raw and underdeveloped with his hand usage, a truth echoed in his almost non-existent sack production. Ball flashed at the Senior Bowl as well, but he needs to develop his operational skill set and improve his leveraging before he can take starting reps. In the immediate timeline, he's an alignment-versatile depth DT.

name: Preston Hodge
blurb: Preston Hodge projects as a depth CB with nickel utility early on in his career, and presents low-level starting upside down the road. He began his career at the JUCO level in 2021, spending one year at Navarro College before transferring to Liberty. After snaring two interceptions with the Flames in 2023, he transferred to Colorado for the 2024 and 2025 seasons. In 2025, he logged an interception and 13 pass deflections, as well as a strong forced incompletion percentage of 13.2%, per TruMedia. At a lean 6'0", 200 pounds, Hodge has ideal size and short-area quickness, with the foot speed, reactive coil, and fluidity to match, redirect, and plant-and-drive with efficiency overtop breaks. His playmaking at the catch is a verifiable strength, but his game is fairly volatile in both phases. In coverage, he can be a tick late to react at times, and can be prone to over-setting and losing leverage in press-man, and he doesn't have the high-end long speed to insulate recovery. Meanwhile, as a support defender, he's woefully inconsistent with his pursuit angles and tackling technique, and struggles to engage blocks properly at times, too. Hodge's athletic ceiling and support limitations hamstring his value as a primary nickel DB, but his experience outside and inside insulates his value as a rotational CB in off-man and zone-heavy schemes.

name: Pat Coogan
blurb: Pat Coogan is a late-round center prospect with a scheme-specific profile but intangible qualities that help raise his floor. He first began his career with the Notre Dame Fighting Irish in 2021. He redshirted his first season and played sparingly in 2022, then started 13 games at left guard in 2023. He began the 2024 campaign as a reserve, but was thrust into the starting center spot due to injuries, and ended up being a crucial contributor amidst Notre Dame's CFB Playoff run. In 2025, Coogan transferred to Indiana and anchored the Hoosiers' National Championship-winning offensive line, earning second-team All-Big Ten and Rose Bowl Offensive MVP honors in the process. At 6'5", 311 pounds, with just over 31" arms, Coogan has a decent size profile, but lesser proportional length, and he's an average athlete at best. Nevertheless, Coogan acquits himself well with experience-forged two-phase awareness, technical composure, and physicality. In the run game, he's heavy-footed and slow to redirect, but is an efficient operator within his sphere, who can prevent displacement with his anchor and core strength, swivel to seal off gaps, and torque through extensions. In pass protection, Coogan is similarly equipped to win in a phone booth; he's patient but decisive with his hands, and has excellent anchor footwork against power. Coogan's limitations ultimately deflate his stock a bit; he has poor balance post-engagement and the farther reps go on, he's not explosive, quick, or rangy as an athlete, and his slow-footed mobility impedes lower-body power drive. Those same balance limitations also cause him to become lopsided and volatile with his pad level and alignment at times. Coogan might be relegated to gap and duo schemes with his limitations, but his guard-center versatility makes him quality depth early on, and he could eventually field reps as a scheme-specific spot starter at center.

name: Aamil Wagner
blurb: Aamil Wagner is a developmental OT prospect who could hear his name called on Day 3 of the 2026 NFL Draft. Wagner redshirted his first season at Notre Dame and became a key reserve in 2023, before serving as the full-time starter at right tackle in 2024 and 2025. In 2025, Wagner also earned team captain honors, and was a semifinalist for the Wuerffel Award for his presence in the community. A locker room leader with two years of starting experience, Wagner will draw NFL interest with his character and intangibles, and he has some compelling physical tools as well. At 6'6", 306 pounds, with 34 1/2" arms, Wagner has excellent proportional length and the lean mass to hold up at contact, and his 5.01 40-yard dash and 9' broad jump at the NFL Combine corroborate his on-field athleticism. As a linear mover, Wagner is powerful and propulsive, with impressive range in space and as a puller. At the same time, however, Wagner is limited on the lateral plane by concering hip stiffness, and he has very little knee bend or leverage acquisition flexibility as a pass protector. Those issues also exacerbate problems with engagement balance and recovery. Wagner has a decent technical floor and generally has good stunt awareness in pass protection, as well as good angle IQ in the run game. However, his poor leverage and flexibility profile can be easily exploited by comparable athletes with power and a plan, and it could prove to be a liability at the NFL level. Wagner's baseline linear athleticism, raw strength, power, awareness, and physicality set his floor as a competent depth or swing tackle, but he'll need to improve his flexibility and leveraging before he can take starting reps as a natural RT.

name: Tyren Montgomery
blurb: Tyren Montgomery was a star of the Senior Bowl, who could ride dominant Division III production and a strong pre-draft process to a Day 3 selection. Montgomery will be over 25 years old as a rookie, but has played just three full seasons as a wide receiver, and has upside to unearth in a shorter prime with more development. Montgomery was originally a walk-on basketball recruit at LSU in 2019, but a winding road led him to pursue football, first through flag football. His highlights caught the attention of Nicholls State, where he played in 2023. In 2024 and 2025, Montgomery played for John Carroll and was utterly dominant, amassing 176 catches for 2,599 yards and 32 touchdowns. At the Senior Bowl, Montgomery measured in at around 5'11", 190 pounds, with near-32" arms, and used his spry short-area mobility, hardcourt crossover skills, and catch-point elevation to produce quality reps in both 1-on-1s and team drills. As an over-aged prospect without prevalent blocking or special teams value and a need for further refinement as a WR, Montgomery won't be on every team's board. Still, teams that value WRs with natural separation building blocks and three-level upside could benefit from throwing a dart Montgomery's way late in the draft.

name: Noah Whittington
blurb: Noah Whittington began his career at Western Kentucky, eclipsing 600 yards in both his sophomore and junior seasons. In 2023, he transferred to Oregon, but a torn ACL sidelined him after just four games. He would return as a crucial rotational presence in 2024 and 2025, achieving a career-high 829 yards and six touchdowns in his sixth and final season. Whittington will be an older rookie, whose season-ending injury in 2023 might generate pause regarding his medical history. Nevertheless, Whittington's late-career resurgence is impossible to overlook, and past the underlying uncertainty with his evaluation, he's a compelling Day 3 value. At 5'8", 203 pounds, Whittington is dense, well-leveraged, and a reasonably athletic runner with great quickness, patience, and cutting balance when navigating set-up space. He's a well-rounded ball carrier with understated competence as a pass-catcher out of the backfield, and he doubles as a near-elite pass blocking presence on later downs, further insulating his rotational utility.

name: Aaron Hall
blurb: Aaron Hall is a late-round DT prospect with the tools to underscore potential long-term starter development and the character to earn a lasting place in a locker room. Hall enjoyed a five-year career with the Duke Blue Devils, and for the final two years, he was a full-time starter and a team captain. In 2024, he accrued a career-high 3.5 sacks and eight tackles for loss, and in 2025, he exceeded that TFL number with a career-high 11 stops behind the line of scrimmage. Additionally, Hall was a top-ten national DT on PFSN's DT Impact rankings, with a score of 84.5. Per TruMedia, he had a 3.7% TFL percentage in the run game, allowed an average of just 1.09 yards per run stop, and had a near-10% pressure rate. Looking at the film, Hall is a bit more boom-or-bust in run defense than his numbers suggest, and his pass-rush arsenal still requires additional refinement. But the potential is undeniable, and it starts with his physical profile. At around 6'4", 296 pounds, with 33 1/2" arms, Hall has a very good blend of lean mass and proportional length, and at his pro day, he reaffirmed his athleticism with a 4.94 40-yard dash, 32" vertical, 9'7" broad jump, and a 7.2 three-cone. Hall's hyper-elite initial explosiveness is an asset in both phases; in one-gapping looks, he can penetrate upfield in short order and swallow up runners, and in the pass-rush phase, he's shown he can surge and rip up gaps with violent intentions. Even after five years and two as a starter, however, Hall's execution still needs work. His aggression can cause him to void his gap in run defense at times, he lacks a consistent pass-rush plan, and can be gathered after initial moves without readily-available counters. Going further, Hall has below-average pad level and balance, and upright drift can sap at his lower-body load and reduce his effectiveness as a power generator. In the short-term, Hall is quality scheme-diverse depth from 3-tech to 5-tech, and starter upside is present down the road, but to realize that upside, he needs further refinement.

name: Cian Slone
blurb: Cian Slone is a late-round or PFA EDGE prospect with a scheme-specific projection and size limitations, but also qualities that should enable him to hold a place in a rotation. Slone had to take the JUCO route at the beginning of his career, spending two years at American River Junior College. In 2022, as a sophomore, Slone dominated to the tune of 12 sacks and 22 tackles for loss, earning an FBS opportunity at Utah State. Across two seasons with the Aggies, he was a full-time starter, and achieved an FBS career-high 7.5 sacks and 9.5 TFLs in 2024. 2025 was Slone's lone season at NC State, but he made the most of it, racking up 6.5 TFLs while also putting up a pressure rate over 11%, per TruMedia, and earning an invite to the Senior Bowl. Slone's TFL production is indicative of a few things. Most central to his game is his absolute unhinged intensity; he has one of the best, most consistent motors in the class, and always comes off the snap with 110% effort and energy. Additionally, Slone is an excellent athlete with near-elite burst and pursuit speed, as well as the hyperactive twitch and agility to off-set blockers and squirm through gaps to get upfield. He's also proficient at acquiring leverage and engaging blocks, though contact situations also shed light on his flaws. At 6'3", 239 pounds, with just 31 1/2" arms, Slone has poor mass and length, and that size deficiency shows up as a major hang-up against larger blockers. More powerful linemen can thoroughly displace him on down and front-side lead blocks, and in the pass-rush phase, Slone's combination of relentless urgency and athleticism doesn't always compensate for his middling power and play strength, and he can be tied up as a result. It also bears noting that Slone doesn't quite have elite hip flexibility or bend, which affects his speed-rushing output. Slone may never be a starter, but in odd and hybrid-front schemes, he bears clear value as a depth and rotational player with inspiring run defense utility, range in pursuit, and a motor that never rests.

name: Luke Altmyer
blurb: Luke Altmyer was a four-star recruit out of high school, who ended up starting three seasons for the Illinois Fighting Illini. He closed out his collegiate career with his best season, completing 67% of his passes while throwing for over 3,000 yards, 22 touchdowns, and just five interceptions, while earning a strong PFSN QB Impact score of 85.6. As a prospect, Altmyer visibly lacks any elite traits. His size is close to average, and his arm is only above-average at best. He's not a dynamic creator, nor is he a consistently elite anticipator. That said, he does have very good pre-snap and post-snap vision, he's reasonably accurate, he has good pocket depth discipline and navigation skills, and he's shown he can get the ball out decisively while not placing passes in harm's way. Altmyer likely won't emerge as a long-term starter in the NFL, but he has the well-rounded -- if unspectacular -- profile to be a quality backup candidate on Day 3.

name: Skyler Thomas
blurb: Skyler Thomas is a late-round safety prospect with moderate athletic limitations but also clear utility in specific schemes. Originally a cornerback recruit out of Menlo Park, California, Thomas signed with Oregon State as a three-star talent in 2021. He played special teams and saw rotational reps in 2021, and had his role expanded in 2022, but then missed the entire 2023 campaign to a preseason injury. In 2024, Thomas returned and played in all 12 games, accruing an interception and nine pass breakups. And in 2025, he was a full-time starter at safety, adding 78 tackles, an INT, and four PBUs to his career totals. He also earned a PFSN Safety Impact score of 83.1, and per TruMedia, he allowed just 0.3 yards per coverage snap, as well as a lowly 34.3 opposing QB rating. Thomas' play would earn him an invite to the Senior Bowl, where he proved himself as a playmaking threat against opposing QBs. At around 6'2", 210 pounds, with over 32" arms, Thomas is long and well-built, with good explosive athleticism and half-field range. He sizes up well in support and is a willing competitor coming downhill, and while he experiences lapses in breakdown timing on occasion, he's shown he can properly wrap up and halt solo runners with his length. In coverage, Thomas has two-high and single-high experience, though without high-end vertical speed, he fits best in Cover 2 schemes at the NFL level. Having said that, Thomas counteracts his vertical limitations with excellent processing ability, route recognition, spatial reasoning, and coverage variability working at different depths. Without elite range or hip fluidity, and with some volatility as a tackle seeker, Thomas' ceiling is slightly capped, but he's an experienced, versatile depth player in the immediate timeline, with mid-level starter potential in split-field schemes.

name: Eric McAlister
blurb: Eric McAlister is a polarizing WR prospect in the 2026 NFL Draft, who has tantalizing physical upside if he can put it all together and alleviate off-field concerns. He joined the TCU Horned Frogs in 2024 after a two-year stretch at Boise State that culminated in a 47-873-5 sophomore campaign. In 2024, McAlister averaged almost 20 yards per catch on 39 receptions, and in 2025, he dominated his team's receiving production to the tune of 72 catches for 1,190 yards and 10 scores, as well as a strong PFSN WR Impact grade of 82.7 and a yards per route run figure of 2.87, per TruMedia. There are off-field concerns with McAlister that teams will have to vet; in March of 2024, McAlister was arrested on a charge of aggravated assault with a deadly weapon after allegedly threatening a man with a gun. He pled guilty to a misdemeanor, was available all through the 2024 and 2025 seasons, and has had no public incidents since. If his character evaluation clears, he's a compelling high-upside WR with near-elite vertical athleticism and speed at 6'4", 194 pounds. He flashes good sink and deceleration as a route runner, has the speed and weaving ability to produce at a high clip in the RAC phase, and can box out at the catch point with his length. McAlister still needs further refinement as a separator and can struggle with poor hand technique and drops at times, and he can more consistently play up to his size both as a 50-50 operator and as a blocker. Still, as a vertical catalyst with untapped three-level potential, McAlister has a physical profile and production profile worthy of consideration on Day 3.

name: Al'zillion Hamilton
blurb: Al'zillion Hamilton is a potential late-round or PFA prospect who was productive at the Group of Six level. A former three-star recruit who played all five seasons at Fresno State, Hamilton first entered the full-time starting lineup in 2023, after flashing promise as a redshirt freshman in 2022. Across three years as a starter with the Bulldogs, Hamilton amassed eight interceptions, 26 pass breakups, and six tackles for loss. In 2025, Hamilton snagged a career-high four INTs, while earning a PFSN CB Impact grade of 82.9. Per TruMedia, he also earned a forced incompletion percentage of 14.9%, and only had a missed tackle percentage of 4.5%. With Hamilton, there are clear physical limitations that may cap his ceiling long-term. His Relative Athletic Score of just 4.78 pulls in non-elite vertical speed, average explosiveness, and below-average size at around 5'11", 186 pounds, with just 31" arms. In particular, the speed deficiency shows up on film, as Hamilton is susceptible to being stacked on the vertical plane. Having said all this, there's also a visible functional floor with Hamilton. While he's not particularly fast or explosive at his size, he is sufficiently fluid, agile, and fleet-footed in coverage. He has a good mirror-motor and willing physicality in press-man, and he can also use a variety of techniques in off-man and zone to maintain spatial discipline and hip leverage with targets in his range. He's a proven playmaker at the catch point with good eye discipline and proactivity, and in run support, he's fairly angle sound, willing to engage, and reliable as a tackle convertor. Additionally, as an experienced member of Fresno State's defense, Hamilton displayed the ability to play on the boundary or in the slot. Against NFL athletes, Hamilton's margin for error will be lower, but he presents as a technically-sound, role-diverse, and instinctive cover man with two-phase playmaking value and a physicality that can help him stick around as quality depth.

name: George Gumbs Jr.
blurb: George Gumbs Jr. is a sleeper EDGE prospect in the 2026 NFL Draft, with subpar pass-rush production but undeniable run-game utility and withstanding upside. Gumbs began his career as a tight end at Northern Illinois in 2021, catching four passes in 2022 while also serving as a special teamer. In 2023, Gumbs made the switch to edge rusher and immediately flashed promise, accruing 3.5 sacks and 6.5 tackles for loss. That early production in his first year as an EDGE was enough to earn the interest of SEC programs. He played for the Florida Gators in 2024 and 2025, totaling 7.5 sacks and 14.5 TFLs while starting a combined 13 of 23 games played. Gumbs will turn 24 years old in the fall and doesn't have a vast pass-rush arsenal, but he's also just three years into his career as a full-time EDGE, and the requisite physical traits and intangibles appear to be present. At 6'4", 245 pounds, with near-34" arms, he's long and lean with impressive dimensions, and he has high-end explosive athleticism, as corroborated by a 41" vertical jump. Gumb's searing explosiveness, length, and lean mass combine to afford him venerable raw power capacity, and for a taller, longer EDGE, he has very good leverage acquisition skills and base engagement.
