He excels at aligning his lower body and fully engaging with his length to set the edge and stack blocks in run defense, and when properly applied, his power profile and lower-body drive can generate push in the pass-rush phase. Gumbs has good-not-great bend, and as it stands, he's largely devoid of a counter arsenal, which can render him inert past initial contact. Additional development is needed before Gumbs can be a starter, but he has the run defense utility, motor, and raw tools to serve as rotational depth and a developmental presence, and potential starting upside is present if he can build on his pass-rush skills, refine his attack execution, and maximize his power profile.

name: Zxavian Harris
blurb: Zxavian Harris is a high-upside DT prospect with unmatched physical traits, but also pressing concerns to address. He signed with Ole Miss out of high school as a four-star recruit and entered the starting rotation as a true sophomore in 2023. He served a major role on the Rebels' line from 2023 to 2025, but 2025 was Harris' most productive season. In his final year, Harris logged nine tackles for loss, three sacks, three pass breakups, and a decent PFSN DT Impact score of 80.4. Harris' character demands careful vetting; he has two arrests on his record, one for a DUI and various driving violations, and another for domestic violence and obstructing arrest. And on film, those same character concerns aren't fully alleviated, as his motor can run very hot-and-cold. Still, for teams that give Harris the all-clear off the field, he'll present tantalizing physical upside to mold. At 6'8", 330 pounds, with near-35" arms, Harris has domineering size, mass, and length, to pair with frightening initial explosiveness off the snap. His natural play strength can be overwhelming for displacement-seeking linemen, and he has the athleticism to play all across the defensive line, from 1-tech to 5-tech, and sometimes beyond as a wide-alignment stand-up rusher. Harris' explosiveness is very slow-footed and lumbering, and he's not incredibly flexible at his size, either. In the pass-rush phase, he's still relatively unrefined with his hand usage and counter arsenal beyond initial power, and pad level management is a constant struggle for him. Still, Harris' blend of elite raw explosion, size, and strength readies him for instant NFL contact contention, and even if he isn't a refined two-phase playmaker, he can be a handful as an alignment-versatile gap occluder and space-closer in pursuit. Harris has tantalizing long-term upside and a respectable floor imbued by his raw traits, but those outcomes dictate that he lives up to the bargain off the field and continues developing on it.

name: Alex Harkey
blurb: Alex Harkey will be a 25-year old rookie, but he's uncharacteristically new to playing on the offensive line. He was a tight end and defensive lineman in high school who produced sparingly and was forced to start at the JUCO level. At Tyler JC, he made the transition to offensive tackle midway through the 2021 season, and continued getting his feet wet at guard and tackle across the 2022 and 2023 seasons, first at Colorado and then at Texas State. Staying with the Bobcats in 2024, Harkey earned third-team All-Sun Belt honors at right tackle, and reprised that role with the Oregon Ducks in 2025. When he was healthy in 2025, Harkey proved inconsistent against top-tier competition, but his upside nonetheless stood out, particularly in the run game. As a former TE, his mobility looms large; he's an explosive athlete with elite pulling and second-level range, and he can channel high-level power outputs from his combined explosion and size, as well as finish with physicality. His pass protection and leverage maintenance are both major works-in-progress, and limitations with length and flexibility will likely relegate him inside to guard full-time. Nevertheless, Harkey can serve as position-versatile depth right away, and he has eventual starting upside.

name: Eric Gentry
blurb: Eric Gentry is an incredibly exciting but also unorthodox linebacker prospect, with ultra-rare height, length, and playmaking upside. He kicked off his collegiate career with Arizona State in 2021, accruing five tackles for loss and a sack in his true freshman season. That early production, coupled with his outlier tools, earned him interest from USC via the transfer portal. Gentry joined up with the Trojans in 2022, and was a starter from that point onward. Across his four-year career in Los Angeles, he totaled 226 tackles, 21.5 TFLs, eight sacks, two interceptions, 11 pass breakups, and nine forced fumbles -- with five of the latter coming in 2025. At nearly 6'7", with 35" arms, Gentry has the height and length profile most tackles and edge rushers dream of. That size profile, however, is marred by an outlier weight at the opposite end of spectrum. At just 220 pounds, Gentry is incredibly light and lean, and that lightness unfortunately shows up in ways both passive and active on film. Gentry can be late to process gap fluctuations at times, and he isn't often eager to engage downhill with physicality in the box. When he does engage blocks, he frequently struggles to fully decouple and enter pursuit until he's already been displaced from gaps. Gentry is at his best in space; he's an explosive, agile, and fluid athlete at his size, with the long-strider range to chase down horizontal threats and the wingspan to engulf and wrangle runners in pursuit. That same disruption radius helps him contend at the catch point in coverage, where he has the fluidity to manage space in zone and the plant-and-drive quickness to man up against select slot targets. Gentry's NFL projection will require a creative touch; he's too light to contend in the box or serve as a consistent pass-rush presence, but quicker slot WRs can also win against him. He needs to add mass no matter what, but as a late-round pick, Gentry's situation-specific upside as a hybrid LB bears clear consideration.

name: Riley Mahlman
blurb: Riley Mahlman started 43 games at Wisconsin, playing at right tackle from 2022 through 2024, before spending most of the 2025 campaign at left tackle. Standing at 6'8", 304 pounds, with just 32 1/8" arms, Mahlman has an unorthodox build, without ideal natural leverage or reach. And yet, he's a surprisingly well-rounded, if unspectacular, OT prospect in the late-round or PFA range. For his size, Mahlman has impressive knee bend and flexibility, and can play with controlled lean to acquire leverage at contact. He's a fairly nimble mover with stellar lateral recovery and balance, he flashes visible knockback power on punches and latches, and he has solid hand carriage, nuance, and synergetic feel in pass protection. Additionally, Mahlman's experience has helped him develop his awareness and reactive quickness against stunts and delayed blitzes, and he has a good anchor for his taller build as well. At his size, Mahlman isn't the most flexible on recovery, and he also struggles to fully acquire leverage and drive his base in the run game at times. For that reason, he's better suited in pass-heavy schemes with his skill set. Nevertheless, Mahlman has a sound two-phase profile in gap, duo, and inside zone schemes, and projects as a quality swing tackle with left-right versatility, and replacement-level starter ability at his peak.

name: Barion Brown
blurb: Barion Brown is a late-round or PFA WR prospect with speed, RAC, and special teams value that may insulate his stock late in April. Brown was a highly-touted four-star recruit in the 2022 cycle, who joined up with and played his first two seasons with the Kentucky Wildcats. He immediately produced as a freshman, achieving his career-highs in both receiving yards and touchdowns, but transferred to LSU in 2024 in a bid for increased production. That high-end receiving production never came for Brown, but he still held a regular place in the Tigers' rotation. In 2025, he caught a career-high 53 passes for 532 yards, earned an 80.9 PFSN WR Impact score, and averaged 4.11 additional yards of RAC over expectation, per TruMedia. Even more compelling is Brown's production as a kick returner; he returned five kicks for touchdowns across his career, and was an All-American return specialist in 2023. Brown earned an invite to the Senior Bowl, where his performance was a mixed bag. Brown's 4.4 speed regularly stresses defenders on the vertical plane, but he doesn't have great deceleration capacity or route nuance to pair with his speed, burst and twitch, and his hands and catch-point composure can be inconsistent, with focus drops positioned as a prevalent issue. At just 177 pounds, Brown is wiry and light, without high-end play strength or contact authority in contested situations, and he'll likely always be a space-dominant player who's dependent on schemed RAC targets. In that lens, Brown has rotational appeal at WR as a vertical and RAC weapon, but just as much value will be driven by his high-level kick return ability in the pros.

name: Joe Fagnano
blurb: Joe Fagnano is one of the more compelling sleepers in the 2026 NFL Draft, if you can get past the age factor. Fagnano will be an older rookie as a seventh-year senior, having first joined the Maine Black Bears as a walk-on in 2019. Fagnano earned a scholarship as a true freshman with a strong summer and proceeded to thrive as a fill-in when Maine's starter went down, amassing 17 touchdowns to just three picks in eight games played. He'd remain Maine's starter for three years before transferring to UConn in 2023. A shoulder injury ended his 2023 season prematurely, but he returned with a vengeance in 2024 and 2025. 2025, in particular, was a stellar year for Fagnano; he earned a PFSN QB Impact score of 86.6, and threw just one interception all year while putting up 3,448 yards and 28 touchdowns at almost 70% completion. At 6'3", 223 pounds, Fagnano fits the prototypical mold at QB, and has acceptable arm talent and athleticism. But what will truly help him stick around as a backup and spot-starter is his strong operational framework: He's a good processor, a decisive thrower, a mature decision maker, a nuanced pocket navigator, and a capable anticipatory passer with the toughness to stand in crowds and deliver.

name: CJ Daniels
blurb: CJ Daniels's path at the collegiate level was a winding one. A sixth-year senior, Daniels originally began his career at Liberty. As a true freshman in 2020, Daniels flashed promise with 220 yards and three TDs on just 11 catches, averaging 20 yards per catch. He'd expand his playmaking imprint in 2021, and after an injury-shortened 2022 season, he eclipsed 1,000 yards and 10 TDs in 2023. Daniels transferred to LSU for his first Power conference opportunity in 2024, but a stacked depth chart contributed to his transfer again in 2025, to the Miami Hurricanes. At Miami, Daniels thrived, catching 46 passes for 495 yards and seven scores. At 6'2", 205 pounds, Daniels is a long-limbed, acrobatic catch-point presence whom passers unequivocally trust, but he also has the route running nuance and vertical ability to support role versatility. His age, injury history, and non-elite production will likely temper his stock, but in the mid-to-late Day 3 range, he's a quality target with immediate rotational appeal.

name: Le'Veon Moss
blurb: Le'Veon Moss leverages his background as a high school track sprinter to showcase breakaway speed on the field. He looked tailor-made for outside zone concepts when healthy in 2024, boasting a strong lower half and high-end contact balance, evidenced by an impressive 4.4 yards after contact per carry. However, a season-ending knee injury cut his 2024 breakout short, and he again failed to stay healthy in 2025. With his top-end speed, fluidity, and balance, Moss has flashed NFL-caliber traits, but his durability casts a shadow on his long-term upside, and he's not a proven receiving weapon, either. If he can stay available at the NFL level, he has dynamic upside as a potential volume back, but that's a big question mark that mars his stock.

name: Josh Cuevas
blurb: Josh Cuevas emerged as a redshirt freshman at Cal Poly, catching 58 passes for 678 yards and six touchdowns. That early emergence put him on the radar of Power conference schools, and he transferred to Washington in 2023 as a result. Cuevas was primarily a rotational TE during Washington's Championship runner-up season, but flashed promise as a big-play threat. When Kalen DeBoer left Washington for Alabama, Cuevas was one of many who followed. He soon re-emerged as the team's TE1 in 2025, racking up 37 catches for 411 yards and four scores, earning a PFSN TE Impact grade of 79.6. Cuevas stands out as a well-rounded utility TE, whose value has always first come from his usage versatility and blocking. Despite having a shorter, short-armed frame, Cuevas is an explosive attacker with good compact mass, elite natural leverage, and eye-catching hand power at engagement. He can square up, drive, and displace defenders within multiple roles, and while his shorter arms affect his sustain in pass protection at times, he holds up well there, too. Meanwhile, as a receiver, Cuevas has good stem IQ, zone instincts, and sturdy hand strength through contact. He likely doesn't quite have the flexibility, speed, or length to be a premier TE1, but he's a high-quality TE2 on day one, and could see stretches as a competent starter.

name: DJ Campbell
blurb: DJ Campbell anchored the interior of Texas’ offensive line in 2025, as a 6'3", 321-pound senior who held down the fort at right guard for three full seasons. Campbell overwhelms defenders with a dense, powerful frame and a natural anchor, giving him a high floor as a draft prospect. While he plays with heavy feet and is still polishing his hand placement on strikes, he often relies on raw physical gifts to win at the point of attack. With improved technique and footwork, Campbell has the potential to develop into a dependable, power-driven guard at the next level. However, athletic limitations slightly cap his ceiling, and he projects better as a depth piece in the immediate timeline.

name: Jack Strand
blurb: Jack Strand is a notable sleeper in the 2026 NFL Draft, who's set to play in front of NFL scouts and evaluators at the American Bowl. At 6'5", 240 pounds, Strand quietly has many of the diagnostic qualities sought after in a NFL backup or developmental starter. He's a plus athlete at his size, with a live arm and elite arm elasticity, arm angle flexibility, and off-platform freedom. On top of his strong physical foundation, Strand has shown he can ID coverages quickly and work through progressions, and though his trigger can be a bit faster still, he's capable of operating both in-structure and on the move at a high level. Strand still has room to clean up his mechanics and get more comfortable with faster-paced defenses, but he has very real upside worth banking on in the middle rounds.

name: Domani Jackson
blurb: Domani Jackson has one of the highest ceilings in the 2026 class, but his floor is also frustratingly low for a player with his experience. He was a five-star recruit out of California, who tied the state record in the 100-meter dash with a blistering time of 10.25. Jackson's freak athleticism was his calling card, but a season-ending knee injury slowed his advance at the CFB level. After an up-and-down sophomore season at USC in 2023, Jackson transferred to Alabama in 2024, where he produced his best tape yet, by far. Jackson's 1-of-1 athleticism shines on tape. He can match anyone and everyone with his hyperactive twitch, foot speed, and reactive coil, and his deep speed ensures no one can stack him in trail. Outside of upside, however, he still leaves a lot to be desired. His severe lack of discipline and situational awareness, as well as an inconsistent motor, lost him starting reps in 2025, and relegated him to a rotational role down the stretch. And his playmaking at the catch point was never a strength across his entire career. Jackson will likely be a potential Day 3 reclamation project in the 2026 NFL Draft, but he has distant starting upside if the right environment can tone down his most detrimental qualities.

name: Dan Villari
blurb: Dan Villari is one of the more intriguing sleepers in the 2026 NFL Draft TE class. A dual-threat quarterback in high school, Villari first committed to play for the Michigan Wolverines, but transferred to Syracuse ahead of his third collegiate season. At Syracuse, Villari steadily made the transition from QB to tight end -- at first finding the field more as a gadget player and utility presence behind Oronde Gadsden II, before stepping into a starting role in 2025. Though the Orange's passing attack took a hit after losing Steve Angeli at QB, Villari was steady and efficient, catching 39 passes for 412 yards, while earning a strong 81.2 PFSN TE Impact score. Full stop: Villari has an argument to be the best route runner in the TE class. He's explosive, fleet-footed, and flexible as an athlete, with elite hip sink and stemming IQ, and he has enough speed to work up seams and box-out crashing safeties for tough catches. He also flashes exciting RAC potential with his vision and energetic leg churn, and has been used as a designed carry threat in wildcat packages. Villari is still underweight and needs to improve his play strength as a blocker, but he has the separation skills to make an instant impact in the passing game and underlie starting upside, and his willingness and leverage acquisition bode well for his run-game development.

name: Jaydn Ott
blurb: Jaydn Ott looked like a future early-round pick after a true sophomore season at California that saw him amass 1,315 yards and 12 touchdowns on 246 carries, earning first-team All-Pac-12 honors in the process. Ott's efficiency tanked in 2024, however, and a transfer to Oklahoma quickly proved fruitless; with the Sooners, Ott exited the summer buried on the depth chart, and barely saw the field in his final season. At his best, Ott proved he's an exciting talent. He's quick and explosive, with a particular affinity for north-south running with his instinctive gap processing and vertical speed, and he's flashed high-level receiving versatility and RAC ability before. But NFL evaluators will have to vet his stark regression over the past two seasons, and even at his peak, he's lacking in terms of physicality, peripheral vision, and pass blocking utility. As it stands, Ott has immense buy-low potential on Day 3, with the natural talent to grow into a quality rotational sparkplug, but pressing questions to answer as well.

name: Behren Morton
blurb: Behren Morton started at least four games in four of his five collegiate seasons, and was a full-time starter when available in his final three years. Though his career ended with a two-interception dud against the Oregon Ducks in the CFB Playoff Quarterfinal, Morton at least produced enough quality tape to make his way onto the NFL radar. At under 6'2" and just over 200 pounds, Morton isn't quite a protoypical passer, nor does he have the desired athleticism or high-end arm strength. His durability has also been a recurring issue over the past few years. Those factors alone, when combined with his inconsistent post-snap processing and accuracy, likely set his ceiling as an NFL backup and spot-starter. That said, Morton at least has the arm elasticity to launch from different arm angles and layer pace and touch on throws, and he has some platform versatility in spite of his middling athleticism. If he can keep refining his mental game and accuracy, he can carve out a respectable career.

name: Desmond Reid
blurb: Desmond Reid is a dynamic potential Day 3 option in the 2026 NFL Draft. He began his collegiate career at Western Carolina and was one of the FCS' most prolific playmakers in 2023, amassing 897 yards and 13 touchdowns on 131 carries. He brought that same impact to Pittsburgh in 2024, racking up 1,545 total yards from scrimmage and returning a punt for a touchdown, a feat he repeated in 2025. On offense, Reid's 2025 campaign was less productive; a nagging lower-body injury limited his availability and efficiency. But if his medicals check out, Reid has the potential to be a vital rotational presence and return specialist. He's explosive, agile, and fluid as a mover, with high-level cutting balance, processing speed, and peripheral vision, and he flashes elite receiving upside, both as a vertical seam-splitter on wheels and streaks, as well as on designed RAC plays. He's more proficient in space than he is navigating through congestion, and overall, he's lacking in the way of size, physicality, and pass blocking utility. Nevertheless, as a two-phase rotational catalyst and return presence, Reid has definite Day 3 appeal.

name: Xavier Nwankpa
blurb: Xavier Nwankpa came into Iowa City with a great deal of hype as a five-star recruit, and flashes in 2023 hinted at greater upside in 2024. Instead, Nwankpa's early-season struggles cascaded, and he lost reps on the defensive side of the ball down the stretch. He had his bright spots and flashed his tantalizing athletic upside, but there remains a need for greater consistency and further technical refinement as Nwankpa enters his fourth season. A move from strong safety to free safety should help for the 6'2", 215-pound playmaker; he thrived as a middle-field ball-hawk in high school with 16 career HS picks. However, improvement with pedal and weave technique will be key in helping Nwankpa reach his ceiling.

name: J'Mari Taylor
blurb: J'Mari Taylor took his talents to Virginia in 2025 after serving as a standout presence at NC Central, and he quickly became a central force in Virginia's ACC runner-up bid. On the year, Taylor amassed 1,062 yards and 14 TDs on 222 carries, while earning a respectable PFSN RB Impact score of 77.8. Taylor's production highlights his steady floor. At around 5'9", 204 pounds, he's not a domineering size threat, nor does he have high-end size-adjusted athleticism, but he's quick to react, instinctive as a spatial creator, and he's incredibly tenacious, with the natural running leverage, contact balance, lower-body drive, and unhinged physical edge to consistently finish forward in congested situations. Taylor doesn't have great hip flexibility on cuts, and this -- combined with his lack of high-end speed and burst -- limits his two-phase upside. He's also a liability as a pass blocker in his current state, which further limits his three-down appeal. That said, Taylor's high functional floor as a ball carrier grants him late-round appeal as a rotiational short-yardage and spell-volume back, who could exceed his capital in a run-heavy role and a beneficial supporting structure.

name: Jalon Daniels
blurb: Jalon Daniels spent six seasons at Kansas, and helped catalyze the Jayhawks' resurgence under Lance Leipold. At his best, Daniels was a star in Lawrence, but his legacy in totality is a more complicated topic, both as a collegiate producer and NFL Draft prospect. He started six games as a true freshman in 2020, before sharing the starting role with Jason Bean through 2021. In 2022, Daniels emerged as the full-time starter, winning five of his nine games started while accruing over 2,000 passing yards and 25 total touchdowns. Daniels' 2022 season ended early due to injury, however, and a severe back injury that featured multiple re-aggravations sidelined him for most of 2023. Daniels returned to the fold in 2024 and 2025, but never quite returned to peak form. Medicals will be a major sticking point for Daniels, and even then, he's largely a middling prospect without high-end raw talent or operational utility. At the very least, he has a wealth of starting experience to fall back on, is a quality athlete with solid arm elasticity, and can effectively operate in the quick-game or on misdirection plays. A slower clock on layered reads and a noticeable cap on maximum arm velocity limits his ceiling, but he's worthy of a camp spot and could carve out a career as a backup in a scheme where he can utilize his dual-threat appeal.

name: Patrick Payton
blurb: Patrick Payton arrives at LSU as a fifth-year senior and one of college football's most experienced edge defenders, bringing 31.5 career tackles for loss and 16 sacks after starting 26 games at Florida State. Towering at 6'6", 255 pounds, Payton stands out for his long, twitchy frame and the ability to anchor the edge with length and power, shutting down run lanes and using his burst to disrupt upfield. While still raw as a pass rusher, he’s easily projectable thanks to his physical tools, outstanding pursuit range, and flexibility to flatten around the arc. If he can continue developing his hand usage and add strength, Payton has the upside to become an early-round NFL Draft pick.

name: Haynes King
blurb: Haynes King was a high school star for the Longview Lobos, and was a highly-touted four-star recruit in the 2020 recruiting class. He signed with Texas A&M and was anticipated as the successor to Kellen Mond in 2021, but a right leg injury cut his season short just two games in. In 2022, King started five games for the Aggies, but won just one of those outings and proved volatile under center. He'd ultimately transfer to Georgia Tech, where he'd get a second wind under Brent Key. In his first season with the Yellow Jackets, King started all 13 games and helped lead Tech to their first bowl victory since 2016. In 2024, he reached career-high efficiency marks as a passer, and in 2025, he passed for almost 3,000 yards while accruing a PFSN QB Impact score of 86.4, as well as almost 1,000 yards and 15 touchdowns on the ground. King rushed for almost 2,500 yards and 37 TDs in his career, a figure that serves as a premonition for his future as an aspiring pro. He has a live arm, which imbues him with measured upside as a passer, but he's ultimately a very limited processor with withstanding mechanical volatility. With his elite creation capacity and steely competitive toughness, King could have value as a potential positional convert, but his upside as a QB is likely limited to that of a QB3 or a low-end backup.

name: Diego Pavia
blurb: Diego Pavia was one of the stars of the 2025 college football season. He began his collegiate career at New Mexico Military Institute after being overlooked as a high scool recruit, and leveraged a dominant second season at NMMI into an opportunity with the New Mexico State Aggies. With the Aggies, Pavia distinguished himself as a dynamic two-phase playmaker, and eventually ascended to the SEC as Vanderbilt's field general. In 2025, Pavia was a Heisman finalist, and earned the top PFSN QB Impact score in the nation, with a grade of 94.8. Pavia's analytics inspire hope; he's an efficient, fairly accurate passer who avoids ill-advised risks, and he boasts a juiced-up and gritty running style. However, his on-field evaluation does leave questions. His processing isn't quite NFL-caliber, nor is his arm strength, and even his athleticism might not be as prevalent against NFL talent. Pavia has the desired mobility, toughness, and quick-game utility to function as an NFL backup, but his ceiling is likely capped beyond that.

name: Rahsul Faison
blurb: Rahsul Faison ran for almost 3,000 yards and 42 touchdowns as a high school senior, but academic issues derailed his early college career. He opted to take the JUCO path after greyshirting at Marshall -- first spending a year at Lackawanna College, before moving to Snow College in 2021. In 2022, Faison saw the field and produced at an efficient clip, and at last began cultivating FBS interest again. At Utah State in 2023 and 2024, Faison totaled 1,845 yards and 14 TDs, before transferring to South Carolina for his final season. With the Gamecocks, Faison was mainly a rotational back, who was kept out of pass blocking duties more often than not, but his raw talent nonetheless flashed. At almost 220 pounds, Faison has unique explosive athleticism and elite hip flexibility and bend as a mover, and working in space, he shows glimpses of high-level peripheral vision and creative freedom, with a vast move arsenal at his size. Faison will be a 26-year old rookie with an uncertain floor and ceiling, but as a late-round or PFA addition, he has the rare creative upside and receiving versatility to be a short-tenure gem in the right environment.

name: Jack Velling
blurb: Jack Velling enters his senior year at Michigan State coming off a 36-411-1 receiving line in 2024. At 6'4", 256 pounds, Velling excels as a vertical seam threat and middle-of-the-field target, leveraging his sizable catch radius and toughness working through traffic to consistently find soft spots in zone coverage. While he lacks top-end speed and acceleration, limitations that can leave him stuck in man coverage, Velling’s sure hands, ability to track passes, and knack for uncovering in space make him a reliable, high-IQ weapon. He profiles as a dependable chain-mover and steady red-zone target with the skill set to carve out an NFL role as a complementary tight end.

name: Joey Aguilar
blurb: Joey Aguilar spent his first four collegiate seasons at the JUCO level, before earning the interest of Appalachian State while at Diablo Valley College. He entered the 2023 campaign as the Mountaineers' backup, but was quickly elevated due to injury and exploded onto the FBS stage. He threw four touchdowns in relief in his first game, took ranked North Carolina to double overtime in his first start, and ended the year with 3,757 yards, 33 TDs, and just ten interceptions. After a slight regression in 2024, Aguilar transferred to Tennessee in 2025, intent on proving his mettle in the SEC. The results were mixed; he logged a solid PFSN QB Impact score of 85, and passed for 3,565 yards, 24 TDs, and 10 INTs, showing off his big arm and big-play ability in the process. However, the Tennessee offensive structure complicates his NFL projection as a processor, and inconsistent down-to-down operation and discernment working the intermediate range proved to be a concern. Of the late-round and PFA options at QB in the 2026 NFL Draft, Aguilar has one of the strongest arms. He flashes easy layering ability up seams and against 1-on-1 coverage, and he's tough and composed when threatened with contact. However, when he wasn't able to isolate 1-on-1s and had to read coverages on the fly, his consistency waned and throws were forced at times. An over-aged project passer with arm talent and toughness to mold, Aguilar is worthy of a camp and QB3 spot, but has an uncertain long-term projection.
